EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recycled moisture underneath a ridge of high pressure was enough to fuel isolated to scattered showers across western Colorado. As these showers decayed they produced gusty outflow winds with some gusts meeting high wind criteria in Mesa County. Two semi trucks rolled over along Interstate 70 as a result of the high winds and shut down portions of the interstate for a short while.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by a trained observer's automated weather station 5 miles northwest of Mack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Substantial monsoon moisture remained over the area as high pressure drifted east over the Plains. As a result, widespread showers and thunderstorms developed with many storms producing heavy rain. This led to several reports of flash flooding and debris flows across portions of west-central and southwest Colorado. Additionally, severe thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds in Mesa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated to be the size of quarters accumulated on the road near Skyway on the Grand Mesa. The accumulation was estimated to be 6 inches deep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fire began in mid June due to lightning 80 miles northwest of Craig and grew to 3,429 acres before it was contained in early July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample moisture remained centered across western Colorado as a shortwave disturbance approached the region. Additionally, residual moisture from previous days convection helped to prime the atmosphere which led to an uptick in shower and thunderstorm coverage. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows across portions of the Pine Gulch Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minor debris flow that was approximately 100 feet wide and around 2 inches deep occurred as a result of heavy rain over the northern portion of the Pine Gulch Fire burn area. Radar estimated 0.20 to 0.50 inches of rain fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed over portions of the I-25 corridor in El Paso and Pueblo Counties during the afternoon, and then spread eastward to southeastward across the Highway 50 corridor through the evening. Some of the thunderstorms were strong to severe, with the more intense storms producing large hail of 1-2 inches in diameter and damaging wind gusts of 60-70 mph. Additionally, a tornado and wind gusts up to around 90 mph were observed across the eastern plains on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles are down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened slightly across portions of west-central Colorado in response to areas of above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation during the month of June. Conditions improved slightly across portions of southwest Colorado. Elsewhere, the drought status remained unchanged through the end of June as a result of the aforementioned conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained largely unchanged across the forecast area during the month of June. Extreme (D3) conditions persisted across the western portion of the zone and expanded slightly to the northeast. Meanwhile, severe (D2) drought continued over the central and northeast portions while the moderate (D1) category lingered over the southeast areas. As a result of the ongoing drought, the town of Norwood is down to less than 17% drinking water left and ranchers are selling off cattle as there is not enough hay to keep the numbers up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged monsoon pattern persisted across much of the region the last couple of weeks of July. This led to cooler and unsettled conditions and brought some much needed precipitation to the Western Slope. As a result, drought conditions improved by one or more categories across many zones by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved across some of the zone during the month of July. Exceptional (D4) drought over the southern tier of the zone improved to the extreme (D3) category as well as over the north-central portion of the zone. The exceptional drought continued over the central portion of the zone and expanded to include the far northeast corner. The extreme drought that was in place over the northwest portion of the zone at the beginning of July remained unchanged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture pushed northward across western Colorado as the ridge of high pressure tilted eastward. As a result, precipitable water values exceeded an inch over the southern and central zones. Given the increase in midlevel moisture, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over the region. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows including across portions of the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Miguel Sheriff's Office reported a tree and mud over Colorado Highway 145 as a result of heavy rains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter-sized hail accumulated in a yard on the east side of Norwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel shortwave trough trekked across the Northern Rockies beneath a weakening ridge. This feature tapped into the already present monsoonal moisture to produce scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. The stronger storms produced gusty winds in excess of 60 mph across portions of west-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 MPH was measured by the automated Mesonet weather station 2 miles southwest of Parachute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prolonged North American Monsoon pattern prevailed across western Colorado with abundant moisture present as precipitable water values trended to 170% of normal. Additionally, morning sunshine allowed the atmosphere to rapidly destabilize over the higher terrain. As a result, another round of strong thunderstorms developed across the region. One storm in Garfield County produced a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted roughly in the vicinity of West Mountain in a remote area northwest of Eagle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure retrograted in response to a deepening mid-level trough over the center of the nation and parked over western Colorado. Even so, subtropical moisture remained entrenched over the area with precipitable water values exceeding an inch and trending over 150% of the climatological norm. As a result, widespread showers and thunderstorms erupted over western Colorado. Additionally, convergence along the Continental Divide enhanced storm coverage. Heavy rain and training was common with storms which led to numerous reports of flash flooding and debris flows, including over the Pine Gulch and Grizzly Creek Fire burn scars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of water ran across Colorado Highway 145 between mile markers 86 and 88.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture remained in place across the Western Slope. As a result, another round of widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across the area with many storms producing heavy rain. This led to several reports of flash flooding and debris flows across portions of west-central and southwest Colorado. Additionally, severe thunderstorms produced large hail in Norwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Miguel Sheriff reported that Sanborn Park Road was impassable and closed due to mudslides from heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 20 miles south of Steamboat Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Muddy Slide Fire was started by lightning 20 miles south of Steamboat Springs in mid June. The fire quickly spread and grew to 4,093 acres before finally being contained in mid September.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 15 miles south of Eagle near Sylvan State Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sylvan Fire began in mid June due to lightning, 15 miles south of Eagle near Sylvan State Park, and grew to 3,792 acres before it was contained in mid October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prolonged North American Monsoon pattern prevailed across western Colorado with abundant moisture present as precipitable water values trended to 170% of normal. Additionally, morning sunshine allowed the atmosphere to rapidly destabilize over the higher terrain. As a result, another round of strong thunderstorms developed and produced heavy rain which caused several mudslides across portions of west-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rockslide occurred as a result of heavy rain along Colorado Highway 82 at Shane Bluffs (mile marker 36).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable environment brought scattered thunderstorms producing frequent cloud-to-ground lightning to the San Juan Mountains in Southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three firefighters working during the mopping up stages on the Johnny Canyon Fire east of Baldy Mountain were caught in a thunderstorm near the 9500 foot level. The thunderstorm intensified overhead with heavy rain, hail and continuous lightning. The crew decided to hunker down in place and wait for the storm to pass. After hearing a large thunderclap, the crew decided they needed to move down the mountain to a safer location. While hiking out, the crew passed through a metal fence gate when one firefighter saw a lightning strike very close by. He saw it split between a tree and the metal fence and immediately felt tingling and cramping in his feet and lower back. Another firefighter was in the process of latching the metal fence gate when he felt a jolt go through his harm and his arm tingling. There were no serious injuries between the three firefighters and all refused treatment, even though they were shivering badly and were experiencing the early stages of hypothermia from being exposed to the heavy rain and hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recycled moisture underneath a ridge of high pressure was enough to fuel isolated to scattered showers across western Colorado. As these showers decayed they produced gusty outflow winds with some gusts meeting high wind criteria in Mesa County. Two semi trucks rolled over along Interstate 70 as a result of the high winds and shut down portions of the interstate for a short while.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colorado State Patrol reported two semi trucks rolled over off of Interstate 70 between exit 11 towards Mack and exit 2 towards Rabbit Valley. The accidents occurred between 9:30 and 9:54 PM MDT. Automated sensors recorded peak wind gusts between 58 and 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple lightning strikes caused a wildfire in a remote area, 23 miles south of Rangely.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oil Springs Fire was started by lightning and was approximately 23 miles south of Rangely. It grew to 12,613 acres before it was contained in mid July. Colorado Hwy 139 was closed from Fruita to north of Douglas Pass for a while due to the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A landspout developed in southwest Weld County, 2 miles southwest of Firestone. The tornado traveled north-northeast, and ended 3 miles northwest of Platteville. The tornado reached an intensity of EF-2 on the Enhanced Fujita scale, with estimated wind speeds of 99 mph.  There were no injuries, but at least two calves were killed, and several chickens.  The cloud base was so high, the landspout was visible from a distance of 40 miles. ||In total, seven properties sustained damage.  Two homes were destroyed, meaning more than 50% of the home sustained damaged, with an estimated loss of those homes at $429,176  Three homes were damaged but not destroyed, with the estimated cost of damage at $101,336.  Two businesses were hit, a feedlot and a dairy. The feedlot suffered the loss of two buildings, one was caused by a downed power line and the other from the tornado. Downed power lines led to a closure of Weld County Road 21 between Highway 66 and Weld County Road 32.5.  The dairy was hit but had limited damage to roofs and some out buildings. The estimated cost of damage to those businesses had not yet been determined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down in southern Weld County.  There were no injuries, but at least two calves were killed, and several chickens. Several homes lost windows and roofing material. One home lost an entire wall and most of its roof, and an unattached garage was completely destroyed with the car inside flipped on its roof. Nearby outbuildings sustained damage, and one caught fire due to downed power lines. Downed power lines also led to a closure of WCR 21 between Highway 66 and WCR 32.5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Substantial monsoon moisture remained over the area as high pressure drifted east over the Plains. As a result, widespread showers and thunderstorms developed with many storms producing heavy rain. This led to several reports of flash flooding and debris flows across portions of west-central and southwest Colorado. Additionally, severe thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds in Mesa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to 4 to 5 inches of standing water in a driveway northwest of Fruita.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recycled moisture underneath a ridge of high pressure was enough to fuel isolated to scattered showers across western Colorado. As these showers decayed they produced gusty outflow winds with some gusts meeting high wind criteria in Mesa County. Two semi trucks rolled over along Interstate 70 as a result of the high winds and shut down portions of the interstate for a short while.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 MPH was measured by the Utah State Line automated weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clouds dissipated in the wake of a cold front which led to strong radiational cooling across western Colorado. This led to subfreezing temperatures in many of the lower valleys where vegetation was sensitive to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 28 to 32 degrees with a low temperature of 30 degrees recorded at the Montrose Regional Airport. The coldest temperature of 22 degrees was measured by an automated station in Olathe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the multi-day event, multiple bands of light to moderate snowfall rates moved across parts of east-central Colorado. Up to 8.5 inches of snow was reported from these bands, with the highest amounts over Yuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow over two days produced 2.2-8.5 inches of snow across Yuma County, Colorado. The greatest amount of snow fell on Monday. The highest total snowfall accumulations were observed near Wray, Colorado. Key impacts: transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened slightly across portions of northwest Colorado in response to areas of above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation during the month of April. Elsewhere, drought status remained unchanged through the end of April as a result of the aforementioned conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of April. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions persisted across the southwest portion of the zone while the extreme (D3) drought remained in the central portion of the zone through the end of the month. The severe (D2) category remained across the far northeast portion of the zone through the end of April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds moved in behind a cold front during the night of the 13th.  As the night progressed the gusts remained strong but decreased below 60 MPH.  During the day on the 14th the winds increased to near or above 60 MPH sporadically through the day then declined some that night.  On the 15th a few sporadic wind gusts around 60 MPH occurred by early afternoon then declined.  However there were a few high wind gusts reported in Cheyenne County.  The peak gust for the event was 76 MPH near Firstview.||These winds also caused a dust storm during the day on the 15th.  Near zero visibility was reported in Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties.  I-70 was closed from Limon to the Kansas border due to multiple crashes west of Kit Carson County.  A car accident at the intersection of Hwy 287 and 40 blocked the highway for six hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds caused a dust storm to occur across the county.  Visibility ranged from a mile or so to almost nothing.  The blowing dust caused a car accident at the intersection of Hwy 40 and 287.  No injuries were reported.  The accident blocked the highway for about six hours.  Key Impacts:  dust storm, blowing dust, reduced visibility, brownout, road closure, vehicle accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds moved in behind a cold front during the night of the 13th.  As the night progressed the gusts remained strong but decreased below 60 MPH.  During the day on the 14th the winds increased to near or above 60 MPH sporadically through the day then declined some that night.  On the 15th a few sporadic wind gusts around 60 MPH occurred by early afternoon then declined.  However there were a few high wind gusts reported in Cheyenne County.  The peak gust for the event was 76 MPH near Firstview.||These winds also caused a dust storm during the day on the 15th.  Near zero visibility was reported in Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties.  I-70 was closed from Limon to the Kansas border due to multiple crashes west of Kit Carson County.  A car accident at the intersection of Hwy 287 and 40 blocked the highway for six hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds caused a dust storm to occur across most of the county.  Visibility ranged from two and a half miles to almost nothing.  I-70 was closed from Limon to the Kansas border due to multiple crashes west of Kit Carson County in the dust storm.  Key Impacts:  dust storm, blowing dust, reduced visibility, brownout, road closure, vehicle accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite the near normal precipitation for East Central Colorado, the D3 Extreme Drought conditions continued for the most part.  Observed precipitation ranged from a tenth to a half inch, with eastern parts of Yuma County approaching an inch of precipitation for the month.  The well above normal precipitation for eastern Yuma County caused the drought status to improve to D2 Severe Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts ranged from 0.25-0.50 for the month.  Normal precipitation values range from 0.10-0.50.  Key Impacts:  drought.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The jet rotating through the base of an upper level low to the west nosed over the Four Corners region. Strong winds mixed down to the surface with some gusts meeting high wind criteria over the Four Corners/Upper Dolores River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by the Dove Creek automated weather station.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clouds dissipated in the wake of a departing system which led to strong radiational cooling across west-central Colorado. This led to subfreezing temperatures in the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 26 to 32 degrees across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry and windy conditions, combined with critically dry vegetation, led to an uncontrolled agricultural burn that became a wildfire approximately 10 miles southeast of Meeker.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 8 Road Fire began from human-causes on April 11th 10 miles southeast of Meeker and grew to 890 acres before being contained several days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary slowly pushed north across eastern Colorado during the afternoon hours before shifting east into western Kansas and extreme southwest Nebraska during the evening hours. Storms formed along and north of the front and moved across eastern Colorado producing tornadoes, up to golf ball sized hail, and up to 60 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed and moved across northern Kit Carson County, producing estimated 60 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle pulse of monsoon moisture worked its way northward across western Colorado. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide resulted in the closure of Colorado Highway 133 just north of Redstone at mile marker 55. The road opened to one lane later that morning before crews finished cleanup operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across portions of Adams, Elbert, and Lincoln counties.  Hail from quarter to ping pong ball size was observed.  Near Limon and Messex, the hail completely covered the roadways.  Two tornados also touched down briefly in Lincoln County but no damage was observed.  Very heavy rain developed across the northeast plains.  The heavy rain caused small stream flooding across southeast Logan and southwestern Phillips counties.  The heavy rain and flooding forced the closure of State Highway 59, south of Haxtun.  Storm totals ranged from 2 to 6 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down in an open field.  No damage was observed.

A large ridge of high pressure dominated the western United States for several days. This resulted in scorching heat and dry weather across the lower elevations of western Colorado. Conditions were exceptionally dry which translated to single digit humidity at the surface. Record breaking high temperatures occurred through the period with overnight lows only dropping into the low to mid to upper 60s and 70s in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures of 100 to 107 degrees were reported by various automated weather stations each day through the period. Additionally, overnight lows ranged from 70 to 73 degrees on the morning of the 8th and 9th. The Grand Junction Regional Airport set a new all-time record high on July 9th with a high of 107 degrees, breaking the old all-time record of 106 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of heavy snow moved into Yuma and Kit Carson counties from the west during the evening of the 15th.  Due to the high snowfall rates, visibility fell to less than a mile at times during the event.  The highest snowfall amount of eight and a half inches was reported in eastern Yuma County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved into east-central Colorado and resided in the area for several days. Wind chills warmed during the daytime before dropping to or below Wind Chill Warning criteria during the night. Area overnight wind chills dropped below -25 degrees Fahrenheit with -44 degrees Fahrenheit being the minimum wind chill recorded during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight variable sustained winds with speeds ranging up to 8 to 14 mph combined with overnight minimum temperatures as cold as -33 degrees Fahrenheit produced wind chills as low as -42 degrees Fahrenheit across Yuma County. The coldest wind chills were observed at the AWOS one mile south-southeast of Yuma, Colorado during the morning of February 15th. Impacts: extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air moved into the High Plains following a winter weather system that brought up to 8.5 inches of snow to northeast Colorado over a span of three days. West-southwesterly to northeasterly winds sustained up to 15 mph caused wind chills as low as 25 degrees below zero to occur during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northeasterly winds up to 10 mph combined with cold temperatures as cold as 11 below produced wind chills as low as 25 degrees below zero across Cheyenne County. The coldest wind chills were observed near Aroya, Colorado. Key impacts: cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds moved in behind a cold front during the night of the 13th.  As the night progressed the gusts remained strong but decreased below 60 MPH.  During the day on the 14th the winds increased to near or above 60 MPH sporadically through the day then declined some that night.  On the 15th a few sporadic wind gusts around 60 MPH occurred by early afternoon then declined.  However there were a few high wind gusts reported in Cheyenne County.  The peak gust for the event was 76 MPH near Firstview.||These winds also caused a dust storm during the day on the 15th.  Near zero visibility was reported in Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties.  I-70 was closed from Limon to the Kansas border due to multiple crashes west of Kit Carson County.  A car accident at the intersection of Hwy 287 and 40 blocked the highway for six hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several rounds of wind gusts near or above 60 MPH during this timeframe, with most gusts between 60 and 70 MPH.  The high winds did cause a dust storm during the 14th.||Key impacts:  high winds, dust storm, blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and associated cold front progressed across the region. As a result, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across western Colorado. Moderate instability produced some stronger thunderstorms that generated severe wind gusts as well as outflow boundaries that led to high winds downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 65 MPH was measured by the Pinto automated weather station 18 miles southeast of Massadona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 20 miles south of Steamboat Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Muddy Slide Fire was started by lightning 20 miles south of Steamboat Springs in mid June. The fire quickly spread and grew to 4,093 acres before finally being contained in mid September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clouds dissipated in the wake of a cold front which led to strong radiational cooling across western Colorado. This led to subfreezing temperatures in several northern and central valleys where vegetation was sensitive to damage. The Upper Yampa River Basin saw a season-ending freeze with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 28 to 32 degrees across the area, including a low of 28 degrees at the Craig-Moffat Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and associated cold front progressed across the region. As a result, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across western Colorado. Moderate instability produced some stronger thunderstorms that generated severe wind gusts as well as outflow boundaries that led to high winds downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 75 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Meeker-Coulter Field Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moved over the Rockies during this period and supported stronger winds across parts of southern Colorado, especially the higher terrain. Snowfall amounts were limited, however, some isolated higher amounts did occur around Monarch Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station La Veta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved into east-central Colorado and resided in the area for several days. Wind chills warmed during the daytime before dropping to or below Wind Chill Warning criteria during the night. Area overnight wind chills dropped below -25 degrees Fahrenheit with -44 degrees Fahrenheit being the minimum wind chill recorded during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight variable sustained winds with speeds ranging up to 13 to 15 mph combined with overnight minimum temperatures as cold as -33 degrees Fahrenheit produced wind chills as low as -44 degrees Fahrenheit across Kit Carson County. The coldest wind chills were observed at the ASOS four miles south of Burlington, Colorado during the morning of February 15th. Impacts: extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure dominated the western United States for several days. This resulted in scorching heat and dry weather across the lower elevations of west-central Colorado. Conditions were exceptionally dry which translated to single digit humidity at the surface. Record breaking high temperatures occurred through the period with overnight lows only dropping into the low to mid to upper 60s and 70s in the Grand Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures of 102 to 105 degrees were reported by various automated weather stations each day through the period. Additionally, overnight lows ranged from 68 to 75 degrees on the morning of the 17th and 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike most likely ignited a wildfire 4 miles south of Akin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Beaver Tail Fire began in early June, most likely due to lightning, 4 miles south of Akin and grew to 463 acres before it was contained a few days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of moisture advected north into the region underneath a ridge of high pressure. Residual moisture from previous days convection helped to prime the atmosphere which led to an uptick in shower and thunderstorm coverage across western Colorado. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized flash flooding across Montrose County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes were flooded with water, mud and debris in the Coal Creek area with 2 feet of water estimated over Kiowa Road between 4 and 6 PM MDT on the 24th. One home saw water levels up to 4 feet with significant damage to the residence and property. Several roads had minor damage from mud and cutting of the dirt in areas. Small rocks and boulders were displaced on steep drainages. Rainfall estimates from local residents varied, but averaged near 1.75 inches in less than 2 hours. No homes were declared a total loss and there were no injuries of note or fatalities. The flash flood was, in addition to the heavy rain, the result of overwhelmed canals and culverts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample moisture remained situated over western Colorado for several days as the backside of an upper level trough extended along the Continental Divide. Additionally, residual moisture from previous days convection helped to prime the atmosphere which led to an uptick in shower and thunderstorm coverage across the area. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows across the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow occurred as a result of heavy rain over the western portion of the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar. The debris flow was about 60 to 70 feet wide over the westbound lanes of Interstate 70 and approximately 5 feet deep. A slide at the bottom of the drainage ranged from 6.5 to nearly 8 feet deep. Radar estimated 0.30 to 0.40 inches of rain fell in 20 to 30 minutes. I-70 westbound was closed for several hours due to cleanup operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample moisture remained situated over western Colorado for several days as the backside of an upper level trough extended along the Continental Divide. Additionally, residual moisture from previous days convection helped to prime the atmosphere which led to an uptick in shower and thunderstorm coverage across the area. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows across the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several debris flows occurred as a result of heavy rain over the western portion of the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar. One debris flow covered both lanes on Interstate 70 and was approximately 80 feet wide and about 5 feet deep. It also ran all the way down to the Colorado River. Radar estimated 0.40 to 0.60 inches of rain fell in 36 minutes. I-70 was closed in both directions for 12 to 24 hours due to cleanup operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours, a large line of storms moves across eastern Colorado producing a swath of damaging wind gusts up to 100 mph across Yuma, Kit Carson, and Cheyenne counties. These strong winds caused damage to trees and powerlines in Yuma county. The greatest amount of damage was in western Yuma County where damage costs estimated around 1 million dollars due to the number of power poles blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the line of storms moved over the location, 1 inch in diameter tree limbs were snapped off trees and a few shingles blown off the roof of a home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trail Canyon Fire burned on Ute Mountain Ute Tribal lands in Montezuma County, CO. Pushed by strong winds, it grew to approximately 840 acres on the afternoon of June 19th. It was started by lightning and is approximately 10 miles NW of Red Mesa, Colorado. The fire grew to 881 acres before it was contained near the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak disturbances trekked across western Colorado. Favorable northerly winds led to strong orographic snow showers over the Elkhead, Park and Gore Mountain ranges. Winds gusted up to 55 mph at times. Additionally, steep lapse rates and instability associated with the secondary wave led to stronger snow bands along portions of Colorado Highway 40. Winds likely gusted to 30 mph with a snow squall that impacted the highway from Hayden towards Steamboat Springs. Visibilities were reduced to a quarter mile or less in the squall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area, much of that in 12 hours or less. Winds gusted from 45 to 55 mph at times.

Winds likely gusted to 30 mph with a snow squall that impacted the highway from Hayden towards Steamboat Springs. Visibilities were reduced to a quarter mile or less in the squall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall moved through the area with the automated sensor at the Yampa Valley Regional Airport in Hayden recording visibility of 1/4 mile or less and a peak wind gust of 30 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong and slow moving area of low pressure impacted the region for several days. As the low slowly lifted northeast into the eastern side of the state, significant moisture wrapped around the low and advected into the area, favoring the southern and central portions of the Western Slope. Moderate to heavy snow fell in the mountains with some ranges estimating almost 20 inches of snow. Winds gusted up to 50 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 19 inches of snow fell at Douglas Pass with webcams showing significantly reduced visibility in blowing snow and slick roads throughout the storm. Winds gusted up to 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed off the Colorado Rocky Mountains during the afternoon hours, moving east-southeast across Eastern Colorado. As the storms moved into western Cheyenne County strong winds, gusting up to 70 mph, caused damage to construction barrels and signs along Highway 287 and downed a 2 inch tree limb on Highway 94.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a line of thunderstorms moved over Highway 94 near the town of Aroya, a 2 inch tree branch was snapped off a nearby tree and found laying on the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of dry weather followed a series of winter storms that brought heavy snow to the mountains of Central Colorado during February and early March. This dry period allow the snowpack to become thin and weak in areas. A pair of backcountry skiers accidentally triggered an avalanche near Lime Creek south of Edwards, CO.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pair of backcountry skiers unintentionally triggered a soft slab avalanche near Lime Creek south of Edwards, Colorado. The avalanche ran about 1000 feet and was 3 feet deep and 25 feet wide. Both skiers were caught with one fatality. Please visit the Colorado Avalanche Information Center's website for additional information on this incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong and slow moving area of low pressure impacted the region for several days. As the low slowly lifted northeast into the eastern side of the state, significant moisture wrapped around the low and advected into the area, favoring the southern and central portions of the Western Slope. Moderate to heavy snow fell in the mountains with some ranges estimating almost 20 inches of snow. Winds gusted up to 50 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 12 inches estimated at the Independence Pass SNOTEL site. Travel was impacted along Interstate 70 with Vail Pass closing for 7 hours due to the hazardous conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level flow shifted to the west-southwest and then the southwest ahead of an approaching Pacific storm. The storm split into two systems - a closed low tracking over the Desert Southwest and a northern wave which trekked across western Colorado on the night of the 22nd. Moderate to heavy snow fell across the northern mountains. Additionally, instability associated with the trough led to stronger snow bands along portions of I-70. Winds gusted to almost 50 mph with a snow squall that impacted the Dotsero to Wolcott area as visibilities dropped to half a mile. The higher elevations saw wind gusts in excess of 55 mph at times throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 16 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted from 45 to 55 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level flow shifted to the west-southwest and then the southwest ahead of an approaching Pacific storm. The storm split into two systems - a closed low tracking over the Desert Southwest and a northern wave which trekked across western Colorado on the night of the 22nd. A secondary wave on the backside of the trough moved over the southern half of the forecast area on the 24th. This long duration storm brought moderate to heavy snow across the central and southern mountains as well as some valleys. Winds gusted from 45 to 55 mph at times in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a period  of light to moderate snowfall to the foothills of Boulder and Larimer counties. A widespread high wind event occurred, as a powerful cold front rolled through the Interior Northwest and northern Rockies before reaching the Midwest.  Strong and damaging winds developed across north central and northeast Colorado.  A peak wind gust of 116 mph was measured at Breckenridge Ski Area, at an elevation of 12,573 feet in the early morning hours of the 14th.  In the foothills of Boulder and Larimer counties, a large shed blew over and smashed into a parked car in Gold Hill, and the   wind snapped two poles near the Glen Haven General Store leading to power outages in the area. Across metro Denver, a fallen tree brought down power lines and sparked a small grass and shed fire; another tree fell into a house in Centennial.  No injuries were reported. Numerous downed trees and power outages occurred over parts of northeast Colorado.  On the 15th, the Colorado Department of Transportation closed Interstate 70 between Airpark Rd and the Kansas Stateline because of a dust storm that left drivers with zero visibility and led to several crashes. U.S. 287/40 from Limon to Kit Carson was also closed.  A fatal accident on I-70 in Lincoln County, halfway between Genoa and Arriba.  At least one fatality was confirmed, with 15 accident victims transported to Limon hospital.  ||In the mountains and foothills, peak gusts included: 116 mph at Breckenridge Ski Resort, 99 mph near Glen Haven, 94 mph near Crisman, 83 mph near Arapahoe Basin and Blackhawk; 80 mph near Marshall, 79 mph near Berthoud Pass, 78 mph along I-25 near Buckeye and Copper Mountain; 77 mph near Nederland and Wellington; 76 mph near Pinecliffe, 75 mph at Virginia Dale, 74 mph at Walden, 66 mph near Frisco, and 65 mph at Tabernash.||Across the urban corridor and northeast plains of Colorado, peak wind gusts included:  81 mph, 8 miles south of Holyoke; 74 mph near Julesburg, 72 mph near Greeley and Limon; 71 mph near Crook, 69 mph near Centennial and Hugo; 67 mph northwest of Nunn; 66 mph at Akron, Holyoke and near Severance; 65 mph near Eaton, Natural Fort Rest Area and near Wiggins; 64 mph in Broomfield, along U.S. 85 near the Wyoming Stateline and Crow Creek; 63 mph near Cedar Point, Elizabeth and Northglenn; 62 mph at Fort Morgan, near Pawnee Pass, Strasburg and Sterling; 60 mph in Burdett, near Buckley AFB, Castle Pines, Lone Tree and Parker; 59 mph near Littleton and Nunn; 58 mph near Briggsdale, Denver International Airport, and Ovid.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gusts included:  99 mph, 1 mile north of Glen Haven; 94 mph, 1 mile northeast of Crisman; 80 mph, 3 miles northwest of Marshall; 77 mph near , 4 miles east-northwest of Nederland and 1 mile northwest of Wellington; 76 mph, 3 miles east-southeast of Pinecliffe; 75 mph at Virginia Dale.

Strong forcing aloft and ample moisture produced moderate snow over southwest Colorado, including both the higher elevations and the lower valleys. Winds in the lower elevations gusted to 35 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive Pacific trough trekked across western Colorado. Mild southwest flow ahead of the storm kept temperatures well above normal before the trough moved through during the overnight hours. Rain fell in the valleys before changing over the snow. Moderate snowfall totals were reported over portions of the northern, central and southern mountains as well as the San Juan River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread high wind event occurred, as a powerful cold front rolled through the Interior Northwest and northern Rockies before reaching the Midwest.  Strong and damaging winds developed across north central and northeast Colorado.  A peak wind gust of 116 mph was measured at Breckenridge Ski Area, at an elevation of 12,573 feet in the early morning hours of the 14th.  In the foothills of Boulder and Larimer counties, a large shed blew over and smashed into a parked car in Gold Hill, and the   wind snapped two poles near the Glen Haven General Store leading to power outages in the area. Across metro Denver, a fallen tree brought down power lines and sparked a small grass and shed fire; another tree fell into a house in Centennial.  No injuries were reported. Numerous downed trees and power outages occurred over parts of northeast Colorado.  On the 15th, the Colorado Department of Transportation closed Interstate 70 between Airpark Rd and the Kansas Stateline because of a dust storm that left drivers with zero visibility and led to several crashes. U.S. 287/40 from Limon to Kit Carson was also closed.  A fatal accident on I-70 in Lincoln County, halfway between Genoa and Arriba.  At least one fatality was confirmed, with 15 accident victims transported to Limon hospital.  ||In the mountains and foothills, peak gusts included: 116 mph at Breckenridge Ski Resort, 99 mph near Glen Haven, 94 mph near Crisman, 83 mph near Arapahoe Basin and Blackhawk; 80 mph near Marshall, 79 mph near Berthoud Pass, 78 mph along I-25 near Buckeye and Copper Mountain; 77 mph near Nederland and Wellington; 76 mph near Pinecliffe, 75 mph at Virginia Dale, 74 mph at Walden, 66 mph near Frisco, and 65 mph at Tabernash.||Across the urban corridor and northeast plains of Colorado, peak wind gusts included:  81 mph, 8 miles south of Holyoke; 74 mph near Julesburg, 72 mph near Greeley and Limon; 71 mph near Crook, 69 mph near Centennial and Hugo; 67 mph northwest of Nunn; 66 mph at Akron, Holyoke and near Severance; 65 mph near Eaton, Natural Fort Rest Area and near Wiggins; 64 mph in Broomfield, along U.S. 85 near the Wyoming Stateline and Crow Creek; 63 mph near Cedar Point, Elizabeth and Northglenn; 62 mph at Fort Morgan, near Pawnee Pass, Strasburg and Sterling; 60 mph in Burdett, near Buckley AFB, Castle Pines, Lone Tree and Parker; 59 mph near Littleton and Nunn; 58 mph near Briggsdale, Denver International Airport, and Ovid.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included 62 mph along I-76 near Sterling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture remained trapped underneath the inversions across northwest Colorado which led to the development of dense fog in some of the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Lower Yampa River Basin including at the Rangely Airport as visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure trekked across the Four Corners with ample moisture wrapped around the system fueling showers over the southern mountains and valleys, particularly under strong upslope flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 9 inches of snow fell across the area with some locally higher amounts in the foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough dropped into the Great Basin and approached the region which resulted in a tight wind gradient aloft. These stronger winds mixed down to the surface with some gusts meeting high wind criteria over the Roan and Tavaputs Plateaus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 78 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Douglas Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep area of low pressure remained nearly stationary over the Great Basin. Subtropical moisture advected into the region and produced wet, heavy snow over the southwest San Juan Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of wet, heavy snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts near a foot estimated at the Bear Town SNOTEL site. Both Red Mountain and Molas Passes were closed along Colorado Highway 550 as a result of the hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level jet streak remained centered over the Four Corners area. Breaks in cloud cover allowed for deep mixing and breezy conditions at the surface with some gusts meeting high wind criteria in La Plata County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Durango-La Plata County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clouds dissipated in the wake of a broad upper level trough which led to strong radiational cooling across western Colorado. This led to subfreezing temperatures in many of the lower valleys where vegetation was sensitive to damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 28 to 32 degrees with a low temperature of 28 degrees recorded at the Craig-Moffat Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 24 to 29 degrees with a low temperature of 29 degrees recorded at the Steamboat Springs/Bob Adams Field Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel trough lifted across western Colorado and produced scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. Increased instability fueled stronger thunderstorms across portions of southwest Colorado. These stronger storms produced frequent lightning, heavy rain, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced large hail measured to be the size of quarters southeast of Stoner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure trekked across the Four Corners with ample moisture wrapped around the system fueling showers over the southern mountains and valleys, particularly under strong upslope flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of 14 inches or greater estimated by SNOTELs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure trekked across the Four Corners with ample moisture wrapped around the system fueling showers over the southern mountains and valleys, particularly under strong upslope flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 9 inches of snow fell across the area with some locally higher amounts in the foothills.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure trekked across the Four Corners with ample moisture wrapped around the system fueling showers over the southern mountains and valleys, particularly under strong upslope flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the area, much of that in 6 hours or less. Winds gusted up to 33 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive mid-level Pacific trough trekked across western Colorado and produced moderate snow over the northern mountains and some adjacent valleys. Heavier snow bands developed as a result of moderate instability which led to reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 14 inches estimated at the Tower SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 47 mph at times.

CZ_NAME: PUEBLO VICINITY / PUEBLO COUNTY BELOW 6300 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow was observed across southern Colorado during this period, as an upper level system slowly moved west of the region. Highest snowfall amounts during this event occurred across the eastern San Juan Mountains, Southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains, and the Wet Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of 6.0 inches. One east Colorado City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel trough lifted across western Colorado and produced scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. Increased instability fueled stronger thunderstorms across portions of southwest Colorado. These stronger storms produced frequent lightning, heavy rain, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced large hail measured to be the size of pennies west-southwest of Durango. The hail accumulated on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure closed off over the Four Corners region and produced moderate to heavy snow over the northern and west-central Colorado mountains. Winds gusted from 45 to 50 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow fell above 8000 feet with up to 15 inches reported along the top of Grand Mesa. Average amounts ranged from 6 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened slightly across portions of northwest Colorado in response to areas of above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation during the month of May. Conditions improved slightly across portions of southwest Colorado. Elsewhere, the drought status remained unchanged through the end of May as a result of the aforementioned conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of May. Exceptional (D4) drought conditions persisted across the southwest portion of the zone while the extreme (D3) drought remained in the central portion of the zone through the end of the month. The severe (D2) category remained across the far northeast portion of the zone through the end of May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive mid-level Pacific trough trekked across western Colorado and produced moderate snow over the northern mountains and some adjacent valleys. Heavier snow bands developed as a result of moderate instability which led to reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 33 mph at times which led to blowing snow and some hazardous travel conditions along Colorado Highway 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive mid-level Pacific trough trekked across western Colorado and produced moderate snow over the northern mountains and some adjacent valleys. Heavier snow bands developed as a result of moderate instability which led to reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced hail up to quarter size near Castle Rock, along with locally heavy rain and minor street flooding.

A series of strong winter storms brought heavy snow to the mountains of southwest Colorado in late January 2021 with new snowfall amounts up to 35.5 inches reported. Strong winds associated with these storms resulted in heavy snow loading on windward slopes. On February 1st, a group of backcountry skiers accidentally triggered a large avalanche resulting in several fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of backcountry skiers triggered a large avalanche while on a multi-day trip in the mountains near The Nose, Middle Fork Mineral Creek southeast of Ophir. This was a soft slab avalanche that was 1000 feet wide and ran 1500 vertical feet. Four skiers were caught in the avalanche with 3 fatalities. Please visit the Colorado Avalanche Information Centers website for more details regarding this incident.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong winter storms brought heavy snowfall to the mountains of central Colorado during the last week of January and into early February. This resulted in a hazardous and unstable snowpack in the Colorado backcountry. A pair of skiers unintentionally triggered a soft slab avalanche near Marvin's West, East Vail backcountry southeast of Vail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pair of backcountry skiers triggered an avalanche near the top of a large open bowl locally known as Marvin's West or Big Marvin (southeast of Vail, CO). But skiers were caught in the avalanche with one unable to escape the path resulting in a fatality. Please visit the Colorado Avalanche Information Centers website for more details regarding this incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced hail up to golfball size just east of Fort Morgan.  The hail smashed a windshield.
A severe thunderstorm produced hail up to quarter size near Castle Rock, along with locally heavy rain and minor street flooding.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two snowmobilers were caught in an avalanche. One was killed, while the other was partially buried and able to extricate himself. The accident occurred near treeline on a northeast-facing slope at around 11,400 feet.  The avalanche was approximately three-quarters of a mile wide and ran about 400 vertical feet. The rider was swept downhill and buried under two feet of snow. Despite efforts from the group of riders and search and rescue, he was not located until the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler was killed by an avalanche on Ruby Mountain, southeast of Rand.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backcountry snowboarder and a snowmobiler were killed by avalanches in separate incidents on Valentine's Day.  In the first incident occurred near Loveland Pass. |The avalanche ran on an east facing slope east of Mount Trelease at an elevation over 12,000 feet.  He was buried with his head under the snow on the downhill side of a few trees. The second incident occurred on the east face of 11,843-foot Mount Epworth, just west of the Continental Divide and Rollins Pass. The slide began above treeline. The snowmobiler got caught up in the slide and was buried underneath his sled on Pumphouse Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler was killed by an avalanche near Rollins Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A positively tilted trough dug into the Great Basin before quickly progressing across western Colorado. Modest forcing aloft and orographic lift led to enhanced snowfall along the Continental Divide with moderate snow reported in the northern and some central zones. Winds gusted up to 50 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel trough and associated surface cold front progressed through western Colorado. Mild temperatures out ahead of the front led to mostly rain in the valleys with moderate to heavy snow falling in a short period of time above 8000 feet. Additionally, instability associated with the front led to stronger snow bands along portions of Colorado Highway 12. Winds likely gusted to near 45 mph with a snow squall that impacted the Crested Butte area as visibilities dropped to near zero based on webcams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 14.3 inches estimated at the Schofield Pass SNOTEL site.||Additionally, a snow squall moved through the Crested Butte area with the webcams north of Crested Butte capturing a heavy band approaching the city. Within 30 minutes, visibilities dropped to near zero with the squall.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near to slightly below normal temperatures prevailed across much of Colorado during the month of February. Additionally, wetter than normal conditions occurred across many areas, though pockets of below normal precipitation prevailed in some spots. Regardless, drought conditions persisted across the entirety of the western slope with much of the region remaining in either the extreme or exceptional category through the end of the month. However, there was some improvement in drought category near the Continental Divide towards the end of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged for the majority of the area through the end of the month with exceptional (D4) conditions prevailing. An exception was across the southeast portion of the zone where the exceptional drought improved to the extreme (D3) category, and over the far eastern portions of the zone where the extreme drought improved to the severe (D2) category in late February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Pacific shortwave troughs trekked across northwest and west-central Colorado. Cold temperatures aloft paired with favorable orographics led to high snow ratios and heavy snow rates across the higher elevations and some adjacent valleys. Winds gusted to over 50 mph at times which led to hazardous driving conditions in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 2 feet of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts in excess of 30 and 40 inches at the Whiskey Park and Tower SNOTEL sites, respectively. Winds gusted from 45 to 55 mph at times. The heavy, blowing snow led to hazardous travel conditions with Rabbit Ears Pass on Colorado Highway 40 closing for short while.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifted across the Four Corners area. Warm southwest flow out ahead of the system kept temperatures warm in the valleys with some areas receiving rain before all precipitation changed to snow as a strong cold front moved through. Moderate to heavy snow fell in most of the western Colorado mountains as well as in the southern valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of 19 inches measured by the CoCoRaHS observer at Mesa Lakes Lodge and over 20 inches estimated at the Thunder Mountain Lodge webcam. Additionally, the Park Reservoir SNOTEL site estimated 16 inches of snow. Colorado Highway 65 was closed over portions of the Grand Mesa due to avalanche control.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near to slightly below normal temperatures prevailed across much of Colorado during the month of February. Additionally, wetter than normal conditions occurred across many areas, though pockets of below normal precipitation prevailed in some spots. Regardless, drought conditions persisted across the entirety of the western slope with much of the region remaining in either the extreme or exceptional category through the end of the month. However, there was some improvement in drought category near the Continental Divide towards the end of February.

The extreme drought improved to the severe (D2) category across the far northeast portion of the zone by the end of February.

An upper level disturbance moving southeast across the Central Rockies triggered widespread showers and thunderstorms. Strong surface winds in the vicinity of the West Elk Mountains near Crested Butte Colorado, combined with local terrain features, aided in the spin-up of a short-lived landspout tornado that was captured by a storm chaser along Kebler Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser traveling along County Road 12 (Kebler Pass) observed a high elevation landspout tornado near the summit of Purple Mountain. The tornado was estimated to be near 12,600 ft and traveled a short distance along the ridge, before dissipating. Snow could be seen blowing around the base of the rotation as it moved across the ridge.

The storms produced damaging thunderstorms wind gusts to 70 mph, hail up to 3 inches in diameter, and 7 brief tornadoes.  Two the tornadoes in Logan County were rated EF1; they knocked down several power poles along State Highway 61 east of Sterling and near Proctor.

A surface cyclone moved across the Rockies into eastern Colorado during the afternoon hours of the 14th. As the cyclone continued to slowly move into western Kansas, it produced rain and snow showers through the morning of the 15th. The highest snow totals were observed in far west Kit Carson County with a total of 7 inches for the event. Combined with light snow received overnight on March 13th, a total snow accumulation was estimated to be 10 inches near the town of Flagler. The snow produced during the event was wet and caused tree limbs to lean and occasionally snap off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on the 14th, rain gradually transitioned to snow across Kit Carson County as a surface cyclone moved across the area into western Kansas. The full transition to snow occurred after sunset and became heavy at times, with the greatest accumulations occurring in the western half of the county.  Snow accumulations ranged from a trace near Burlington to 7 inches north of Flagler.  This snow as an additional amount from the lighter snow accumulations that occurred in the western half of the county on the evening of the 13th. A trained spotter near Flagler reported an estimated 10 inches from the 13th to the 15th. Key impacts: Slick roadways, heavy snow, reduced visibilities.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow developed over northeast Colorado into the northern mountains and foothills on the 13th, which then intensified overnight.  The heavy snow then gradually shifted southward on the 14th and covered much of the area.  The snow slowly decreased in the evening with only some lingering light snow across the plains continuing into the morning of the 15th.  North winds increased to 25 to 35 mph, on the 14th, with gusts up to 50 mph.  This produced blizzard conditions along the I-25 Corridor from Palmer Divide north to the Wyoming border.   Denver International Airport runways were closed, from noon on 14th until 2 pm on the 15th, with over 2000 flights canceled.  All major Interstates were closed except for those areas around Denver and  Fort Collins.  Extensive drifting snow was observed, with drifts anywhere from 3  to 7 feet deep.  The heavy wet snow caused extensive tree damage and produced power outages. Xcel stated the number of customers in northern Colorado affected by power outages exceeded 15,000. Many were left powerless overnight as crews were unable to reach downed power lines. The majority of the outages were in Weld County. ||Localized amounts up to 4 feet were reported in the mountains and foothills of Larimer County; a trained spotter near Buckhorn Mountain measured an impressive 48.5 inches of snow.  Snowfall amounts in the Front Range Mountains and Foothills ranged from 20 to 40 inches, with 18 to 30 inches reported along the I-25 Corridor.  The official snow for Denver was 27.1 which was the 4th largest storm on record since 1882.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  24 inches near Roxborough Park; 20 inches near Crescent Village and Evergreen; 14 inches Chatfield Reservoir; and 13 inches near White Ranch Open Space.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into Yuma and Kit Carson counties during the early evening.  Behind the front northerly winds gusted up to 60 MPH and blowing dust reduced visibility down to five miles or so.  The strongest winds were reported at the Burlington airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts of 58 MPH were measured near Idalia behind the cold front.  Key impacts:  blowing dust, high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours, a large line of storms moves across eastern Colorado producing a swath of damaging wind gusts up to 100 mph across Yuma, Kit Carson, and Cheyenne counties. These strong winds caused damage to trees and powerlines in Yuma county. The greatest amount of damage was in western Yuma County where damage costs estimated around 1 million dollars due to the number of power poles blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station FIXFARM reported a 61 mph gust as the line of storm move over the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a significant improvement in the drought status for East Central Colorado due to widespread rainfall during the month.  The much needed rainfall caused the drought status to improve to D1 Moderate Drought for most of East Central Colorado.  There was even a small area of D0 Abnormally Dry for eastern Kit Carson County and part of southern Yuma County.  Despite the much needed rainfall, the western half of Cheyenne and far west Kit Carson County only improved to D2 Severe Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts ranged from two to five inches across the county.  The lowest precipitation was over the western half of the county.  Normal values for the month are 0.5-1.  This is one to four inches above normal.   Key impacts: drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow developed over northeast Colorado into the northern mountains and foothills on the 13th, which then intensified overnight.  The heavy snow then gradually shifted southward on the 14th and covered much of the area.  The snow slowly decreased in the evening with only some lingering light snow across the plains continuing into the morning of the 15th.  North winds increased to 25 to 35 mph, on the 14th, with gusts up to 50 mph.  This produced blizzard conditions along the I-25 Corridor from Palmer Divide north to the Wyoming border.   Denver International Airport runways were closed, from noon on 14th until 2 pm on the 15th, with over 2000 flights canceled.  All major Interstates were closed except for those areas around Denver and  Fort Collins.  Extensive drifting snow was observed, with drifts anywhere from 3  to 7 feet deep.  The heavy wet snow caused extensive tree damage and produced power outages. Xcel stated the number of customers in northern Colorado affected by power outages exceeded 15,000. Many were left powerless overnight as crews were unable to reach downed power lines. The majority of the outages were in Weld County. ||Localized amounts up to 4 feet were reported in the mountains and foothills of Larimer County; a trained spotter near Buckhorn Mountain measured an impressive 48.5 inches of snow.  Snowfall amounts in the Front Range Mountains and Foothills ranged from 20 to 40 inches, with 18 to 30 inches reported along the I-25 Corridor.  The official snow for Denver was 27.1 which was the 4th largest storm on record since 1882.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  11.2 inches near Gary; 8 inches around Fort Morgan, with 7 inches near Wiggins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours, a large line of storms moves across eastern Colorado producing a swath of damaging wind gusts up to 100 mph across Yuma, Kit Carson, and Cheyenne counties. These strong winds caused damage to trees and powerlines in Yuma county. The greatest amount of damage was in western Yuma County where damage costs estimated around 1 million dollars due to the number of power poles blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station K2V5 reported a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system slowly moved across the region during this time frame, providing an extended period of moderate to heavy snow across the region. The widespread heavier snow did lessen in coverage towards the end of the period, however, continued to focus across the Palmer Divide. Moderate to heavy snow persisted in this location along with high winds, with blizzard conditions noted across far northern El Paso county. Highest snowfall amounts of around two feet were also observed in this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moved across the region during this period, producing a period of heavy snow across the eastern mountains and far southern CO, especially the southern I-25 corridor. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow developed over northeast Colorado into the northern mountains and foothills on the 13th, which then intensified overnight.  The heavy snow then gradually shifted southward on the 14th and covered much of the area.  The snow slowly decreased in the evening with only some lingering light snow across the plains continuing into the morning of the 15th.  North winds increased to 25 to 35 mph, on the 14th, with gusts up to 50 mph.  This produced blizzard conditions along the I-25 Corridor from Palmer Divide north to the Wyoming border.   Denver International Airport runways were closed, from noon on 14th until 2 pm on the 15th, with over 2000 flights canceled.  All major Interstates were closed except for those areas around Denver and  Fort Collins.  Extensive drifting snow was observed, with drifts anywhere from 3  to 7 feet deep.  The heavy wet snow caused extensive tree damage and produced power outages. Xcel stated the number of customers in northern Colorado affected by power outages exceeded 15,000. Many were left powerless overnight as crews were unable to reach downed power lines. The majority of the outages were in Weld County. ||Localized amounts up to 4 feet were reported in the mountains and foothills of Larimer County; a trained spotter near Buckhorn Mountain measured an impressive 48.5 inches of snow.  Snowfall amounts in the Front Range Mountains and Foothills ranged from 20 to 40 inches, with 18 to 30 inches reported along the I-25 Corridor.  The official snow for Denver was 27.1 which was the 4th largest storm on record since 1882.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  48.5 inches near Buckhorn Mountain; 42 inches, 8 miles northeast of Four Corners; 41.5 inches near Aspen Springs; 41 inches near Pingree Park; 36.5 inches near Horsetooth Mountain; 33 inches, 7 miles southeast of Cherokee Park; 31 inches 7 miles east-northeast of Virginia Dale; 30 inches near Campion, Pinecliffe and 7 miles west of Rustic; 28.5 inches near Jamestown; 28 inches near Laporte; 26 inches near Estes Park and Nederland; 24.5 inches near Glen Haven; 23.5 inches near Berthoud and 23 inches near Eldorado Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual moisture from previous days convection helped to prime the atmosphere which led to an uptick in shower and thunderstorm coverage across western Colorado. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows across the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several debris flows occurred as a result of heavy rain over the eastern portion of the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar. The debris covered both sides of Interstate 70 and ran all the way down to the Colorado River. Hourly rain rates exceeded 0.50 inches/hour. I-70 was closed in both directions for several hours due to cleanup operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Calwood burn areas caused flash flooding from Geer Canyon Drive to CO 36.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced localized flash flooding in the Black Hollow area.  The flooding hampered ongoing recovery work in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough progressed across the region. As a result, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across western Colorado. However, dry low levels led to gusty winds rather than heavy wetting rains with convection. Some storms generated severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Grand Junction Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure trekking over southwest Colorado was absorbed into a broader Pacific trough that lifted across the Western Slope. This led to scattered to widespread showers and thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows across portions of west-central Colorado over the course of a couple of days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported 6 to 8 inches of water and mud flowing across a driveway in a residential area as a result of heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorms produced large hail in central Adams County.  The hail ranged from quarter to golf ball size.

The beginning and end of September saw weather systems move into the region, resulting in cooler and unsettled conditions, most notably for the southern and some central sites. As a result, some areas saw slight improvement in the category of the drought. Other locations remained unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved over some of the zone during the month of September. The exceptional (D4) drought over the northwest portion of the zone was trimmed back slightly while the extreme (D3) drought continued elsewhere. An exception was across the southern tier of the zone which improved to the severe (D2) category by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the July 25th, a lee trough set up over eastern Colorado that allowed for the development of scattered thunderstorms in east-central Colorado during the afternoon hours. These thunderstorms produced hail up to nickel in size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received at location of hail ranging from pea to nickel in size along with torrential rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More than 30 people had to be rescued from Lake Dillon following a microburst, which produced wind gusts as strong as 55 mph.   Paddlers were completely overwhelmed by the sudden and strong wind gusts which flipped them off their boards and kayaks.   Fortunately, Colorado Parks and Wildlife along with the Summit County Sheriff���s Office were already on the water for recovery training.  They quickly picked up stranded boaters from the water and nearby islands, and towed beached boats back to the docks.  No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sudden microburst winds knocked 30 people off their paddle boards and kayaks in Lake Dillon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure trekking over southwest Colorado was absorbed into a broader Pacific trough that lifted across the Western Slope. This led to scattered to widespread showers and thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows across portions of west-central Colorado over the course of a couple of days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Burn scar debris flow from the Pine Gulch Fire flooded a house and rerouted Roan Creek at a bend 15 miles northwest of De Beque. Water marks along South Dry Fork Road were 3 to 6 feet high.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deadly debris flow from the Cameron Peak burn area, struck Black Hollow Gulch west of Rustic on CO-14.  The debris flow, estimated to be 5 to 8 feet deep, destroyed 6 homes and other structures.  Four people were killed when their residence was swept away by the floodwaters.  The houses and structures were at the mouth of Black Hollow Canyon on the Poudre River. The debris consisting of small, medium, and large boulders, mud, and entire burned trees flowed down the canyon and destroyed the houses in that area, including the bridge that crossed the Poudre River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow moved into Black Hollow Gulch and damaged or destroyed several structures including four homes.  Four people in one of the homes perished when their residence washed away in the floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced several short-lived landspouts, large hail, damaging winds.  Five landspouts, all rated EF-0, touched down briefly in portions of eastern Adams and Morgan Counties.  Large hail, up to half dollar size, was observed in eastern Adams, Logan, and Washington counties.  Intense thunderstorm winds, ranging from 60 to 80 mph, were reported in northern Lincoln, western Logan, and southeastern Washington counties.  The winds downed a power line along Logan County Road 34, north of Sterling.   Minor tree damage was observed in northeast Lincoln County.

Heavy rain produced a debris flow that impacted CO-125 near Mile Marker 7 in the East Troublesome burn area. A USFS vehicle was entrapped in a mud/debris flow on Cabin Creek Road. It partially buried his vehicle. The employee was safe and uninjured.  Localized flash flooding also occurred along Kinney Creek in the Williams Fork burn area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced a debris flow that impacted CO-125 near Mile Marker 7.  County Road 21 was also flooded near its confluence.  The debris flow forced the closure of the highway until it could be cleared.  A mud/debris flow in the Cabin Creek drainage partially buried a parked USFS vehicle, and trapped the employee behind a washed-out road. The Forest Service employee was assessing one of the closed forest service roads when the storm hit, he was found safe and uninjured.

One flash flood occurred on Coal Creek during the early morning hours. A nearby spotter measured 5 inches of rainfall during the event. In the next event, a flash flood reportedly produced 3 feet of running water.  The floodwaters moved several vehicles along with some heavy equipment.  Later in the day, severe thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding over portions of Adams and Weld counties.  Heavy rainfall, up to 4.4 inches in one hour, caused flash flooding in Greeley.  The Greeley Fire Department had to close several streets and conducted multiple water rescues.  Large hail up to half dollar size was also reported.  Heavy rain also produced localized flash flooding in the Cameron Peak burn area.  In Adams County, a severe thunderstorm produced a wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred on Tonahutu Creek.  Trees, mud and debris shut down Grand Lake's hydro plant.  Approximately 50 downed trees piled up at the plant's head gate and ash and mud filled the ditch leading to another gate.  Another flash flood impacted CO-125.  Additional flash flooding occurred in the Calwood and Cameron Peak burn areas, where up to 2.5 inches of rain fell in one hour.  In Erie, heavy rain anywhere from 3.5 to 4.5 inches, produced flash flooding.  Several vehicles were stranded in intersections and had to be rescued.  Severe thunderstorms also produced hail up to quarter size in Larimer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding along Tonahutu Creek forced the shut down of Grand Lake's hydro plant.

During the late afternoon on July 9th, storms developed off the Rockies and moved southeast across Northern Colorado and Southern Wyoming. As the storms moved southeast, they clustered together and produced outflow boundaries for more storms to develop along. As the storms moved across East-Central Colorado, they produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 71 mph. In Cheyenne County, Colorado, 51 mph wind gusts were able to produce blowing dusts with near-zero visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Davis weather station on the property recorded a 71 mph wind gust associated with the outflow of an approaching thunderstorm.

At Buckley Air Force Base, a microburst produced a peak wind gust to 68 mph.

Heavy rainfall associated with a thunderstorm in the Cameron Peak burn area, produced flash flooding in Masonville.  Heavy rainfall, up to one inch in an hour, produced flash flooding along Buckhorn Road, south of Big Bear Road.  Running water, up to 6 inches deep, covered the roadway.  Heavy rain also produced minor flooding in the East Troublesome burn area.  Runoff from that storm impacted a small section of Trail Ridge Road along Onahu Creek. A swath of black water, approximately 100 yards wide and 5 inches deep, inundated the roadway briefly and lasted only about 5 minutes. Trail Ridge Road was closed temporarily to clear away the mud.  There were some minor mud and flooding issues on Green Mountain Trail too.  Finally, a severe thunderstorm produced hail up to quarter size in southwest Boulder.
