EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered storms occurred across the eastern mountains and plains, with the highest coverage noted over the far southeast plains. A diminishing trend with this development occurred on this day, however, a few storms did produce heavy rainfall with flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 1.22 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the monsoon plume and moist low level upslope flow allowed for thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and flash flooding to occur on this day across the region. The highest concern for flash flooding was over the mountains and I-25 corridor region, but later in the evening, additional flash flooding occurred along and south of Highway 50.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 4.04 inches. First 3 inches of rain fell in approximately 1 hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced intense winds and large hail across Adams, Broomfield, Douglas, Jefferson, Morgan, Weld and Phillips counties.  The hail ranged in size from one to two inches in diameter.  Three short-lived brief tornadoes touched down in Phillips County.  One caused moderate damage, when it destroyed two outbuildings and rolled over a semi-trailer.  Very heavy rainfall in southeast Phillips County, caused flash flooding the forced closures of US 6 east of Holyoke and several other county roads southeast of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced very heavy rain in southeast Phillips County.  Local law enforcement reported several roads were flooded southeast of Holyoke.  Rainfall measurements from 4 to 9 inches were reported.  Several houses had water rise up to the front doors.  Significant flash flooding was observed 6 miles east of Holyoke on U.S. Highway 6.  Widespread flooding of roads, ditches, and fields was reported. At least one road crossing Frenchman Creek was impassable.  Frenchman Creek and other streams had significant flooding as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall associated with a thunderstorm in the Cameron Peak burn area, produced flash flooding in Masonville.  Heavy rainfall, up to one inch in an hour, produced flash flooding along Buckhorn Road, south of Big Bear Road.  Running water, up to 6 inches deep, covered the roadway.  Heavy rain also produced minor flooding in the East Troublesome burn area.  Runoff from that storm impacted a small section of Trail Ridge Road along Onahu Creek. A swath of black water, approximately 100 yards wide and 5 inches deep, inundated the roadway briefly and lasted only about 5 minutes. Trail Ridge Road was closed temporarily to clear away the mud.  There were some minor mud and flooding issues on Green Mountain Trail too.  Finally, a severe thunderstorm produced hail up to quarter size in southwest Boulder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Masonville. Minor flooding was observed at the Masonville Post Office. There was a debris jam at the bridge. Black Creek Road in the Retreat was washed out in the storm. Both sides of the road were eroded by water flow making the it impassable. Big Bear Road was washed out from the paved road to the gate of nearby property.  A recently installed culvert was washed out, and the road was impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall associated with a thunderstorm in the Cameron Peak burn area, produced flash flooding in Masonville.  Heavy rainfall, up to one inch in an hour, produced flash flooding along Buckhorn Road, south of Big Bear Road.  Running water, up to 6 inches deep, covered the roadway.  Heavy rain also produced minor flooding in the East Troublesome burn area.  Runoff from that storm impacted a small section of Trail Ridge Road along Onahu Creek. A swath of black water, approximately 100 yards wide and 5 inches deep, inundated the roadway briefly and lasted only about 5 minutes. Trail Ridge Road was closed temporarily to clear away the mud.  There were some minor mud and flooding issues on Green Mountain Trail too.  Finally, a severe thunderstorm produced hail up to quarter size in southwest Boulder.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of disturbances moved across western Colorado during the month of October. This led to areas of above normal precipitation for the month which resulted in some improvement of the drought across the region, most notably across the northern zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained largely unchanged across the zone through the month of October. The extreme (D3) drought continued across the southern portion of the zone with severe (D2) conditions over the central areas and moderate (D1) drought elsewhere through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system which was associated with a prolonged atmospheric river event moved across western Colorado. The abundance of moisture associated with this system paired with the strong cold front resulted in widespread snow showers in the higher elevations. Moderate snow fell in most ranges, generally above 9,500 feet. Additionally, gusty winds accompanied the cold front with gusts up to 60 mph recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 50 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared in the wake of a secondary Pacific trough which led to subfreezing temperatures in several valleys where vegetation was sensitive to damage. Some areas saw a season-ending freeze with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 22 to 28 degrees across the area. This was a season-ending freeze for the San Juan River Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong upper level Pacific trough and associated cold front dug into the Great Basin. As it approached western Colorado it resulted in a tightening wind gradient. Winds at the surface gusted in the 40 to 50 mph range for the valleys and in the 60 to 70 mph range for the mountains. There were localized gusts meeting high wind criteria in the Roan Cliffs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 79 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at Douglas Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system which was associated with a prolonged atmospheric river event moved across western Colorado. The abundance of moisture associated with this system paired with the strong cold front resulted in widespread snow showers in the higher elevations. Moderate snow fell in most ranges, generally above 9,500 feet. Additionally, gusty winds accompanied the cold front with gusts up to 60 mph recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with some sites seeing close to a foot of snow. Lesser amounts were recorded across the southern portion of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning to early afternoon hours, an intense low pressure system moved away from the Colorado Front Range and across Eastern Colorado. This system was responsible for generating two rounds of high winds. The high winds gusted between 40 to 110 mph over a period of approximately 5.5 hours. The high winds were able to knock down numerous power poles and damage numerous trees across Cheyenne County. Both rounds of high winds were able to generate dust storm conditions; one before and one along a cold front that moved east-northeast across South-Central Colorado into Northwest Kansas. The high winds were also able to spark a 125 acre wildfire in a corn field that caused damage to an irrigation pivot and power poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of high winds occurred over Cheyenne County. The high winds were able to cause damage to an unknown number of power poles and trees across the county with gusts up to 110 mph. Mesonets across the county recorded wind gusts of 58-84 mph, with the strongest recorded gust located near Firstview, Colorado.

This is near normal for all but the eastern third of the county where the observed precipitation is 0.50-1 below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One flash flood occurred on Coal Creek.  A nearby spotter measured 5 inches of rainfall during the event.  In the next event, a flash flood reportedly moved several vehicles along with some heavy equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced intense winds and large hail across Adams, Broomfield, Douglas, Jefferson, Morgan, Weld and Phillips counties.  The hail ranged in size from one to two inches in diameter.  Three short-lived brief tornadoes touched down in Phillips County.  One caused moderate damage, when it destroyed two outbuildings and rolled over a semi-trailer.  Very heavy rainfall in southeast Phillips County, caused flash flooding the forced closures of US 6 east of Holyoke and several other county roads southeast of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture advected into the region during the month of August. This led to cooler and unsettled conditions and brought some much needed precipitation to the Western Slope. As a result, drought conditions improved by one or more categories across some zones by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of August with abnormally dry to moderate drought (D0 to D1) across the far northeast portion of the zone with severe (D2) conditions in the central portion of the zone. Finally, extreme (D3) conditions persisted across the southwest portion of the zone through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure began to reposition over the Great Basin, cutting off the stream of deep monsoonal moisture over the region. Even so, enough moisture lingered over the area which resulted in another round of diurnal showers and thunderstorms. The stronger storms produced heavy rain which led to localized flash flooding and debris flows in San Miguel, Gunnison and Eagle counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two mudslides occurred on Colorado Highway 145 at mile markers 85 and 86 just north of the junction of Highways 62 and 145. The San Miguel County Sheriff's Office reported two feet of debris across the roadway at mile marker 86.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure began to reposition over the Great Basin, cutting off the stream of deep monsoonal moisture over the region. Even so, enough moisture lingered over the area which resulted in another round of diurnal showers and thunderstorms. The stronger storms produced heavy rain which led to localized flash flooding and debris flows in San Miguel, Gunnison and Eagle counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Cimarrons produced intermittent flash flooding and a debris flow of rocks and branches on National Forest Service Road 861.1 (Middle Fork Road), near the Owl Creek Pass junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual monsoon moisture paired with daytime heating was enough to generate scattered showers and thunderstorms over the mountains of west-central Colorado. Some storms produced moderate to heavy rain which led to localized debris flows in Pitkin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pitkin County Sheriff reported the closure of Independence Pass along Colorado Highway 82 between mile markers 47 and 72 due to mudslides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture across southwest Colorado resulted in another round of showers and thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows in San Juan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide closed Hotel Draw Road (Forest Service Road 550) half a mile east of its intersection with the Colorado Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure originating from the Pacific Northwest dropped into the Great Basin. As this feature slowly lifted across the region it tapped into a stream of monsoon moisture from the south. The upper level lift paired with the abundance of moisture resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide of unknown size temporarily closed down Colorado Highway 13 between County Road 73 and County Road 38.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even with residual cloud cover, much colder air filtered into western Colorado in the wake of a departing early season winter storm. This led to subfreezing temperatures in several valleys where vegetation was sensitive to damage. Some areas experienced a season-ending freeze with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 23 to 28 degrees across the area. This was a season-ending freeze for the Lower Yampa River Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of September 1st, a lee surface trough moved into the east-central Colorado region which caused thunderstorms to develop in the area. As a result, an intense microburst formed near the Burlington Colorado airport. The strong winds associated with this microburst then spread to the north, impacting the eastern portions of the community of Burlington. This storm produced a swath of damage in excess of 4 miles long, with the most intense damage occurring between the airport and Interstate 70. The damaging winds from the microburst were estimated to be up to the 100-115 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense straight line winds due to the microburst first occurred at 8:12 PM MDT near the Burlington airport. This began an area of intermittent damage consistent with wind speeds in the 100-115 mph range. The most intense winds occurred over an area 4.2 miles long and 600 yards wide, ending near Interstate 70 and the Kit Carson Correctional facility. The most significant damage occurred near the Burlington airport where a large evergreen tree was snapped at the trunk and near the Kit Carson Correctional Facility where four power poles were snapped and a nearby outbuilding was destroyed. Damage due to winds in the 60-80 mph range continued to the north of Interstate 70. Additional storm damage included: uprooted trees; damage to the wind and visibility sensors of the KITR ASOS, a camper trailer, overhead garage doors (blown in/dented), fences, windows, siding and roofs of outbuildings and a mobile home; and the flipping of shipping containers and semi-truck trailers. The city of Burlington also lost power as a result of the storm damage.

Throughout the day, a cold front gradually moved into Eastern Colorado, causing wind speeds to increase throughout the afternoon with gusts up to 60 mph across Yuma and Kit Carson counties. During the early afternoon hours, a combine sparked a fire in a cornfield in southern Yuma County. The fire spread quickly due to the strong winds. The fire was contained; however, there was one fatality and one injury due to the flames.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combine sparked a fire in a cornfield near the intersection of County Road T and Highway 36, quickly spreading due to the strong winds gusting up to 60 mph. Volunteer fire fighters responded and began to control the fire when the winds shifted direction due to the cold front. The winds shifted the direction of the flames, killing one firefighter and sent another to the hospital due to burns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed off the Colorado Rocky Mountains during the afternoon hours, moving east-southeast across Eastern Colorado. As the storms moved into western Cheyenne County strong winds, gusting up to 70 mph, caused damage to construction barrels and signs along Highway 287 and downed a 2 inch tree limb on Highway 94.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a line of thunderstorms moved across Cheyenne County, strong wind gusts up to 60 mph scattered construction barrels and signs across Highway 287.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough slowly moved inland across the western United States. Unsettled westerly flow out of this system, paired with a series of embedded disturbances, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow in the higher elevations. A strong cold front produced heavy snow bands, one of which sat over west-central Colorado for several hours. Winds gusted up to 55 mph in the mountains and up to 40 mph in the valleys. Bitterly cold temperatures moved into the region in the wake of the trough's passage on New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across the area.

CZ_NAME: CANON CITY VICINITY / EASTERN FREMONT COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level system and surface low approached the region late in the day on the 14th, and then lifted across Colorado during the day on the 15th. As the system moved overhead a strong upper level jet of 120 KT moved from southwest to northeast across the region, from the San Luis Valley eastward into the eastern Colorado plains. This setup favored heavy mountain snow across the Continental Divide, primarily the eastern San Juan mountain. Meanwhile, lighter amounts of snow were observed across the central and eastern mountains. As this system pivoted across the region, the strength of this system and some weak instability supported the development of snow squalls from the Continental Divide east into parts of the I-25 corridor. The strong jet moving across the region as well as mountain wave action produced widespread extreme winds over much of southern Colorado. The very dry/drought conditions in place and the extreme winds then produced widespread blowing dust over much of the plains.||High/Extreme fire danger was also noted given the strength of the wind field and falling RH values, falling well into the teens, when combined with gusts over 70 MPH. Numerous grass and prairie fires occurred caused by falling power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles leaning across east bound highway 50, which is now closed for safety closure.

Bitterly cold temperatures moved into the region in the wake of the trough's passage on New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 40 mph.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SAN JUAN MOUNTAINS ABOVE 10000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: December 8th began a very active period of heavy mountain snowfall across most of western Colorado, from the Continental Divide westward. Several feet of snow fell across the southwest mountains causing heavy travel impacts on US Highway 160, closing Wolf Creek Pass for a time due to blowing snow and near white out conditions. Heavy snow was also reported along the western side of the Continental Divide, the Sawatch Mountain range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total of 32 inches over the past 48 hours.

CZ_NAME: CANON CITY VICINITY / EASTERN FREMONT COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level system and surface low approached the region late in the day on the 14th, and then lifted across Colorado during the day on the 15th. As the system moved overhead a strong upper level jet of 120 KT moved from southwest to northeast across the region, from the San Luis Valley eastward into the eastern Colorado plains. This setup favored heavy mountain snow across the Continental Divide, primarily the eastern San Juan mountain. Meanwhile, lighter amounts of snow were observed across the central and eastern mountains. As this system pivoted across the region, the strength of this system and some weak instability supported the development of snow squalls from the Continental Divide east into parts of the I-25 corridor. The strong jet moving across the region as well as mountain wave action produced widespread extreme winds over much of southern Colorado. The very dry/drought conditions in place and the extreme winds then produced widespread blowing dust over much of the plains.||High/Extreme fire danger was also noted given the strength of the wind field and falling RH values, falling well into the teens, when combined with gusts over 70 MPH. Numerous grass and prairie fires occurred caused by falling power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The D2 Severe Drought Status expanded a small amount east during December.  D3 Extreme Drought continued over the western portion of Kit Carson.  Observed precipitation ranged from 0.01 to 0.25, which was near normal.

Meanwhile, lighter amounts of snow were observed across the central and eastern mountains. As this system pivoted across the region, the strength of this system and some weak instability supported the development of snow squalls from the Continental Divide east into parts of the I-25 corridor. The strong jet moving across the region as well as mountain wave action produced widespread extreme winds over much of southern Colorado. The very dry/drought conditions in place and the extreme winds then produced widespread blowing dust over much of the plains.||High/Extreme fire danger was also noted given the strength of the wind field and falling RH values, falling well into the teens, when combined with gusts over 70 MPH. Numerous grass and prairie fires occurred caused by falling power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the Pikes Peak region and plains on this day along a surface low and boundary extending from around Teller County southeast through Las Animas County. This served as a focus for severe thunderstorms, with large hail up to one and a half inches noted across the Pikes Peak region, and a tornado observed across Las Animas County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cool and unsettled weather at the end of December resulted in some improvement of the drought across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved across the entirety of the zone through the month of December. The severe (D2) drought over the central portion of the zone improved to the moderate (D1) category with moderate conditions persisting across the far northeast corner. Additionally, the extreme (D3) drought over the southwest portion of the zone improved to the severe category.

An initial shortwave trough and then a larger open wave moved across western Colorado. Strong upper level support, impressive moisture, widescale synoptic lift, and a strong surface cold front resulted in widespread precipitation with moderate to heavy snow accumulating in most mountain areas as well as some valley locations. Winds gusted to 55 mph in the higher elevations and exceeded 30 mph in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 14 inches of snow fell across the area.

A potent upper level system and surface low approached the region late in the day on the 14th, and then lifted across Colorado during the day on the 15th. As the system moved overhead a strong upper level jet of 120 KT moved from southwest to northeast across the region, from the San Luis Valley eastward into the eastern Colorado plains. This setup favored heavy mountain snow across the Continental Divide, primarily the eastern San Juan mountain. Meanwhile, lighter amounts of snow were observed across the central and eastern mountains. As this system pivoted across the region, the strength of this system and some weak instability supported the development of snow squalls from the Continental Divide east into parts of the I-25 corridor. The strong jet moving across the region as well as mountain wave action produced widespread extreme winds over much of southern Colorado. The very dry/drought conditions in place and the extreme winds then produced widespread blowing dust over much of the plains.||High/Extreme fire danger was also noted given the strength of the wind field and falling RH values, falling well into the teens, when combined with gusts over 70 MPH. Numerous grass and prairie fires occurred caused by falling power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough dominated the western United States and kicked out a series of Pacific systems across the region. This wave train impacted western Colorado for several days and produced heavy snow in the mountains as well as very strong winds. Moderate snow also fell in some valleys once colder temperatures arrived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 10 inches reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall with possible flash flooding was the main concern on this day, especially over and near the higher terrain. While the heaviest rainfall was not widespread, there were isolated reports of heavier rainfall over one inch.

A prolonged atmospheric river event brought anomalously high moisture to the Western Slope as a series of Pacific systems lifted across the region. Heavy snow fell in the mountains resulting in hazardous holiday travel with blowing and drifting snow occurring as wind gusts exceeded 60 mph in the higher elevations. Mild southwest flow early in the event resulted in high snow levels before a cold front arrived later on. Moderate snowfall accumulations also occurred in some valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 2 feet of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted from 45 to 65 mph.

A prolonged atmospheric river event brought anomalously high moisture to the Western Slope as a series of Pacific systems lifted across the region. Heavy snow fell in the mountains resulting in hazardous holiday travel with blowing and drifting snow occurring as wind gusts exceeded 60 mph in the higher elevations. Mild southwest flow early in the event resulted in high snow levels before a cold front arrived later on. Moderate snowfall accumulations also occurred in some valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 18 to 36 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 50 inches estimated at the Schofield Pass SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 45 mph.

CZ_NAME: WALSENBURG VICINITY / UPPER HUERFANO RIVER BASIN BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active pattern continued across southern Colorado during this period, with multiple upper level disturbances moving across the region. This supported another long period of damaging winds and heavy snow. Highest wind gusts were in the 70 to 90 mph range, with a 101 mph wind gust observed near Monarch Pass. The heavy snow was not too widespread, though Wolf Creek Pass did observe a storm total snow of 40 inches.

This setup favored heavy mountain snow across the Continental Divide, primarily the eastern San Juan mountain. Meanwhile, lighter amounts of snow were observed across the central and eastern mountains. As this system pivoted across the region, the strength of this system and some weak instability supported the development of snow squalls from the Continental Divide east into parts of the I-25 corridor. The strong jet moving across the region as well as mountain wave action produced widespread extreme winds over much of southern Colorado. The very dry/drought conditions in place and the extreme winds then produced widespread blowing dust over much of the plains.||High/Extreme fire danger was also noted given the strength of the wind field and falling RH values, falling well into the teens, when combined with gusts over 70 MPH. Numerous grass and prairie fires occurred caused by falling power lines.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial shortwave trough and then a larger open wave moved across western Colorado. Strong upper level support, impressive moisture, widescale synoptic lift, and a strong surface cold front resulted in widespread precipitation with moderate to heavy snow accumulating in most mountain areas as well as some valley locations. Winds gusted to 55 mph in the higher elevations and exceeded 30 mph in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 16 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup was not overly favorable for widespread severe weather on this day, however, isolated thunderstorms did develop across the eastern plains of Colorado. A few severe storms did develop, and produced hail to one inch across Pueblo and Kiowa counties.

The setup was not overly favorable for widespread severe weather on this day, however, isolated thunderstorms did develop across the eastern plains of Colorado. A few severe storms did develop, and produced hail to one inch across Pueblo and Kiowa counties.

Thunderstorm development on this day was mainly confined to the I-25 Corridor and westward, with burn scar flash flooding a primary concern on this day. While this was the main concern, an isolated severe storm did occur across the eastern plains, as well as a brief landspout tornado occurring over the San Luis Valley. This tornado was brief, and did not produce any damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged atmospheric river event brought anomalously high moisture to the Western Slope as a series of Pacific systems lifted across the region. Heavy snow fell in the mountains resulting in hazardous holiday travel with blowing and drifting snow occurring as wind gusts exceeded 60 mph in the higher elevations. Mild southwest flow early in the event resulted in high snow levels before a cold front arrived later on. Moderate snowfall accumulations also occurred in some valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms were possible in the afternoon into mid evening on this day, mainly over and near the mountains. However, the high wind gust report was not associated with the thunderstorms but with a heat burst. A rapid rise in temperatures and winds occurred at the National Weather Service Office in Pueblo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat burst occurred at the NWS office as winds gusted to 62 mph and the temperature rose to 95 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough dominated the western United States and kicked out a series of Pacific systems across the region. This wave train impacted western Colorado for several days and produced heavy snow in the mountains as well as very strong winds. Moderate snow also fell in some valleys once colder temperatures arrived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area.

Mild southwest flow early in the event resulted in high snow levels before a cold front arrived later on. Moderate snowfall accumulations also occurred in some valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anonymously strong trough of low pressure dug into the Great Basin. A 170 knot jetstreak resided over western Colorado which resulted in gusty winds at the surface, some of which exceeded high wind criteria in the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather station at the Montrose Regional Airport reported a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough dominated the western United States and kicked out a series of Pacific systems across the region. This wave train impacted western Colorado for several days and produced heavy snow in the mountains as well as very strong winds. Moderate snow also fell in some valleys once colder temperatures arrived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first disturbance generated 3 to 10 inches of snow across the region with 10 to 20 inches of snow from the second system. Storm total snowfall for the area was 1 to 2 feet of snow with up to 33 inches of snow estimated at the Park Reservoir SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 60 mph.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level system and surface low approached the region late in the day on the 14th, and then lifted across Colorado during the day on the 15th. As the system moved overhead a strong upper level jet of 120 KT moved from southwest to northeast across the region, from the San Luis Valley eastward into the eastern Colorado plains. This setup favored heavy mountain snow across the Continental Divide, primarily the eastern San Juan mountain. Meanwhile, lighter amounts of snow were observed across the central and eastern mountains. As this system pivoted across the region, the strength of this system and some weak instability supported the development of snow squalls from the Continental Divide east into parts of the I-25 corridor. The strong jet moving across the region as well as mountain wave action produced widespread extreme winds over much of southern Colorado. The very dry/drought conditions in place and the extreme winds then produced widespread blowing dust over much of the plains.||High/Extreme fire danger was also noted given the strength of the wind field and falling RH values, falling well into the teens, when combined with gusts over 70 MPH. Numerous grass and prairie fires occurred caused by falling power lines.

A potent Pacific trough lifted across western Colorado and brought moderate to heavy snow to many mountain ranges as well as some high valleys. Wind gusts in the lower elevations exceeded 50 mph with gusts of more than 60 mph reported in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted to more than 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cool and unsettled weather at the end of December resulted in some improvement of the drought across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved across the entirety of the zone through the month of December. The severe (D2) drought across the northern portion of the zone improved to the moderate (D1) category while the extreme (D3) drought down south improved to the severe category.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cool and unsettled weather at the end of December resulted in some improvement of the drought across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved slightly across the zone through the month of December. The extreme (D3) drought over the southern portion of the zone was trimmed back slightly to only include the southwest corner. The severe (D2) conditions over the central areas was also trimmed back while the moderate (D1) drought continued elsewhere through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increases in moisture and instability led to a higher coverage of strong to severe storms across the plains on this day. Winds and hail were the main thunderstorm risks as storms moved off the mountains and into the plains during the afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system began to enter the southern tier of Colorado on December 8th providing some high country snows and very strong southwest winds across the southern I-25 corridor during the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough dominated the western United States and kicked out a series of Pacific systems across the region. This wave train impacted western Colorado for several days with a strong cold front accompanying one such system. Instability associated with the front led to stronger snow bands along portions of western Colorado. Wind gusts exceeded 50 mph with a strong snow squall that impacted the Highway 40 corridor from Meeker towards Steamboat Springs as visibilities dropped to near zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the area. A snow squall moved through the area with the automated sensor at the Meeker Coulter Field Airport recording visibility of less than 1/4 mile and a peak wind gust of 55 MPH. This same squall previously impacted the Craig area and produced visibility of less than 1/4 mile and gusts of 40 to 50 MPH for 20 minutes at the Craig-Moffat Airport. The squall continued along Highway 40 and produced less than 1/4 mile visibility and gusts up to 38 mph at the Yampa Valley Regional Airport in Hayden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough dominated the western United States and kicked out a series of Pacific systems across the region. This wave train impacted western Colorado for several days with a strong cold front accompanying one such system. Instability associated with the front led to stronger snow bands along portions of western Colorado. Wind gusts exceeded 50 mph with a strong snow squall that impacted the Highway 40 corridor from Meeker towards Steamboat Springs as visibilities dropped to near zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the area. A snow squall moved through the area with the automated sensor at the Steamboat Springs Airport/Bob Adams Field recording visibility of less than 1/4 mile and a peak wind gust of 45 MPH as it moved from Hayden to Steamboat Springs along Highway 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough slowly moved inland across the western United States. Unsettled westerly flow out of this system, paired with a series of embedded disturbances, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow in the higher elevations. A strong cold front produced heavy snow bands, one of which sat over west-central Colorado for several hours. Winds gusted up to 55 mph in the mountains and up to 40 mph in the valleys. Bitterly cold temperatures moved into the region in the wake of the trough's passage on New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation measured 16 inches of snow at Douglas Pass with plenty of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial shortwave trough and then a larger open wave moved across western Colorado. Strong upper level support, impressive moisture, widescale synoptic lift, and a strong surface cold front resulted in widespread precipitation with moderate to heavy snow accumulating in most mountain areas as well as some valley locations. Winds gusted to 55 mph in the higher elevations and exceeded 30 mph in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific trough lifted across western Colorado and brought moderate to heavy snow to many mountain ranges as well as some high valleys. Wind gusts in the lower elevations exceeded 50 mph with gusts of more than 60 mph reported in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 5 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 14 inches estimated at the McCoy Park SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 60 mph.

An embedded mid level disturbance helped to develop showers and storms across the region through the early evening on this day, with strong to severe storms occurring across El Paso County through the far southeast plains. A broad Pacific trough slowly moved inland across the western United States. Unsettled westerly flow out of this system, paired with a series of embedded disturbances, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow in the higher elevations. A strong cold front produced heavy snow bands, one of which sat over west-central Colorado for several hours. Winds gusted up to 55 mph in the mountains and up to 40 mph in the valleys. Bitterly cold temperatures moved into the region in the wake of the trough's passage on New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 18 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 2 feet estimated at the Lost Dog SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 50 mph.

Unsettled westerly flow out of this system, paired with a series of embedded disturbances, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow in the higher elevations. A strong cold front produced heavy snow bands, one of which sat over west-central Colorado for several hours. Winds gusted up to 55 mph in the mountains and up to 40 mph in the valleys. Bitterly cold temperatures moved into the region in the wake of the trough's passage on New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 18 to 40 inches of snow fell on the southern end of the zone with 8 to 18 inches reported on the north side. Winds gusted up to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level system and surface low approached the region late in the day on the 14th, and then lifted across Colorado during the day on the 15th. As the system moved overhead a strong upper level jet of 120 KT moved from southwest to northeast across the region, from the San Luis Valley eastward into the eastern Colorado plains. This setup favored heavy mountain snow across the Continental Divide, primarily the eastern San Juan mountain. Meanwhile, lighter amounts of snow were observed across the central and eastern mountains. As this system pivoted across the region, the strength of this system and some weak instability supported the development of snow squalls from the Continental Divide east into parts of the I-25 corridor. The strong jet moving across the region as well as mountain wave action produced widespread extreme winds over much of southern Colorado. The very dry/drought conditions in place and the extreme winds then produced widespread blowing dust over much of the plains.||High/Extreme fire danger was also noted given the strength of the wind field and falling RH values, falling well into the teens, when combined with gusts over 70 MPH. Numerous grass and prairie fires occurred caused by falling power lines.

A deep upper level trough dominated the western United States and kicked out a series of Pacific systems across the region. This wave train impacted western Colorado for several days and produced heavy snow in the mountains as well as very strong winds. Moderate snow also fell in some valleys once colder temperatures arrived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first disturbance generated 6 to 12 inches of snow across the region with locally higher amounts of up to 16 inches estimated at the Whiskey Park SNOTEL site. The second system produced 3 to 10 inches of snow across the area. Storm total snowfall for the area was 10 to 20 inches of snow with over 2 feet of snow estimated at Whiskey Park. Winds gusted up to 87 mph.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough dominated the western United States and kicked out a series of Pacific systems across the region. This wave train impacted western Colorado for several days and produced heavy snow in the mountains as well as very strong winds. Moderate snow also fell in some valleys once colder temperatures arrived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first disturbance generated 4 to 8 inches of snow across the region with 5 to 10 inches of snow from the second system. Storm total snowfall for the area was 10 to 20 inches. Winds gusted up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough dominated the western United States and kicked out a series of Pacific systems across the region. This wave train impacted western Colorado for several days and produced heavy snow in the mountains as well as very strong winds. Moderate snow also fell in some valleys once colder temperatures arrived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first disturbance generated 6 to 12 inches of snow across the region with locally higher amounts of up to 15 inches estimated at the Schofield Pass SNOTEL site. The second system produced 6 to 14 inches of snow across the area with an estimated 34 inches at Schofield Pass. Storm total snowfall for the area was 1 to 2 feet of snow with over 50 inches of snow estimated at Schofield Pass. Winds gusted up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cool and unsettled weather at the end of December resulted in some improvement of the drought across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved slightly across the zone during the month of December. The extreme (D3) drought over the northern portion of the zone was trimmed back slightly. The severe (D2) conditions elsewhere improved to the moderate (D1) category over the far southeast portion of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of cool and unsettled weather at the end of December resulted in some improvement of the drought across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved over much of the zone through the month of December. The extreme (D3) drought over the northern tier of the zone was trimmed back to only include the northwest corner. The severe (D2) conditions down south were trimmed back as it improved to the moderate (D1) category along the southeast corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the Pikes Peak region and plains on this day along a surface low and boundary extending from around Teller County southeast through Las Animas County. This served as a focus for severe thunderstorms, with large hail up to one and a half inches noted across the Pikes Peak region, and a tornado observed across Las Animas County.

Abundant monsoon moisture remained in place across the Western Slope. As a result, another round of widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across the area with many storms producing heavy rain. This led to several reports of flash flooding and debris flows across portions of west-central and southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both lanes of Colorado Highway 50 were closed near mile marker 115 as a result of a mudslide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prolonged North American Monsoon pattern prevailed across western Colorado with abundant moisture present as precipitable water values trended to 170% of normal. Additionally, morning sunshine allowed the atmosphere to rapidly destabilize over the higher terrain. As a result, another round of strong thunderstorms developed and produced heavy rain which caused several mudslides across portions of west-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide and flooding occurred on Red Mountain Pass as a result of heavy rains. Colorado Highway 550 was closed in both directions at the pass for a couple of hours due to cleanup operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure retrograted in response to a deepening mid-level trough over the center of the nation and parked over western Colorado. Even so, subtropical moisture remained entrenched over the area with precipitable water values exceeding an inch and trending over 150% of the climatological norm. As a result, widespread showers and thunderstorms erupted over western Colorado. Additionally, convergence along the Continental Divide enhanced storm coverage. Heavy rain and training was common with storms which led to numerous reports of flash flooding and debris flows, including over the Pine Gulch and Grizzly Creek Fire burn scars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large mudslide occurred across Colorado Highway 145 near Sawpit as a result of heavy rains. The highway was closed for a couple of hours due to cleanup operations. The most severe slide occurred at mile marker 79 with 1 to 2 feet of debris and boulders across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure retrograted in response to a deepening mid-level trough over the center of the nation and parked over western Colorado. Even so, subtropical moisture remained entrenched over the area with precipitable water values exceeding an inch and trending over 150% of the climatological norm. As a result, widespread showers and thunderstorms erupted over western Colorado. Additionally, convergence along the Continental Divide enhanced storm coverage. Heavy rain and training was common with storms which led to numerous reports of flash flooding and debris flows, including over the Pine Gulch and Grizzly Creek Fire burn scars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple debris flows occurred along Interstate 70 within the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar between mile marker 123 and Hanging Lake Tunnel. Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT) described the damage from the new flows as extreme. The new debris piles were around 17 feet tall over the roadway and house-sized boulders fell as well. Hanging Lake trail received considerable damage. At least seven bridges were either destroyed, washed downstream or otherwise severely damaged. National Forest officials say it will be at least late-season 2022 before the trail can be reopened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even with high pressure centered over the region, deep monsoonal moisture continued to advect into western Colorado. In addition to the well above normal precipitable water values across the area, the passage of a subtle shortwave trough resulted in the development of widespread showers and thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to debris flows including across portions of the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains resulted in a debris flow of mud, branches and rocks across Avalanche Creek Road. The road remained closed and impassable overnight before reopening the next morning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in urban flash flooding south-southwest of Rifle. A trained weather spotter reported gutters overflowing and flooding on roadways.

This led to several reports of flash flooding and debris flows across portions of west-central and southwest Colorado. Additionally, severe thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds in Mesa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be in the 40 to 50 mph range knocked down a portion of a wooden fence in a neighborhood in Clifton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture pushed northward across western Colorado as the ridge of high pressure tilted eastward. As a result, precipitable water values exceeded an inch over the southern and central zones. Given the increase in midlevel moisture, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over the region. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows including across portions of the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: San Miguel Sheriff reported several mudslides in the east end of San Miguel County along Colorado Highway 145 Spur towards East Colorado Avenue. The road was closed for several hours due to cleanup operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture pushed northward across western Colorado as the ridge of high pressure tilted eastward. As a result, precipitable water values exceeded an inch over the southern and central zones. Given the increase in midlevel moisture, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over the region. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows including across portions of the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least three debris flows of mud, trees and rocks occurred across the eastern portion of the Grizzly Creek Fire burn area near Bair Ranch. Radar estimated 0.30 to 0.50 inches of rain fell in 20 minutes over the affected area. I-70 was closed in both directions for 24 hours due to cleanup operations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain formed over the Grizzly Creek burn scar near Glenwood Springs, CO which caused a major mudslide across the Colorado River near MM 124 of Interstate 70. According to CDOT, the material in the river resulted in some damming of the river. Additionally, a smaller mudslide occurred at MM 129 at the same time and flowed across I-70 with an unknown depth. I-70 was closed for an extended period due to cleanup operations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broader midlevel trough moved across western Colorado and acted on ample residual monsoonal moisture to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows across portions of the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two minor debris flows occurred across the southern portion of the Grizzly Creek Fire burn area. One to three inches of very slick mud ran across Interstate 70 at mile marker 128 off Cinnamon Creek from the south side of Glenwood Canyon. Mud also ran underneath the railroad tracks north of I-70 in this section. Radar estimated 0.20 to 0.50 inches of rain fell across the area. I-70 was closed in both directions for several hours due to cleanup operations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mudslides occurred along Colorado Highway 133 near Redstone as a result of heavy rains. Debris depth was 7 to 8 feet with one slide and 3 to 4 feet deep with another. As a result, motorists were stranded until cleanup operations were complete.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Substantial monsoon moisture remained over the area as high pressure drifted east over the Plains. Additionally, strong instability was in place as a midlevel shortwave trough moved overhead. As a result, widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across the area with many storms producing heavy rain. This led to multiple debris flows across portions of west-central Colorado including over the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar. Additionally, a severe thunderstorm produced large hail in Garfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flash flooding and debris flows occurred throughout Glenwood Canyon from the Grizzly Creek Fire burn scar as a result of significant rainfall from showers and thunderstorms. Multiple mudslides were reported by the Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT). Numerous vehicles were trapped between slides for several hours. Rainfall rates in excess of 4 inches/hour were estimated with storms. Remote gauges recorded between 1 and 2 inches in less than an hour along the south and east sides of the burn scar. CDOT reported damage to the structure of Interstate 70 which remained closed for over two weeks due to cleanup operations and surveys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prolonged North American Monsoon pattern prevailed across western Colorado with abundant moisture present as precipitable water values trended to 170% of normal. Additionally, morning sunshine allowed the atmosphere to rapidly destabilize over the higher terrain. As a result, another round of strong thunderstorms developed and produced heavy rain which caused several mudslides across portions of west-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A section of Hotel Draw Road (Forest Service Road 550) was blocked by a mudslide one-half mile east of its intersection with the Colorado Trail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mudslides occurred as a result of heavy rains along Colorado Highway 133 near Redstone and Swiss Village. The highway was closed for a couple of hours due to cleanup operations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains led to a debris flow across sections of First Fork Road on the Pagosa Ranger District. Portions of the road were washed out. The exact time of the slide is unknown and was estimated based on surrounding observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure retrograted in response to a deepening mid-level trough over the center of the nation and parked over western Colorado. Even so, subtropical moisture remained entrenched over the area with precipitable water values exceeding an inch and trending over 150% of the climatological norm. As a result, widespread showers and thunderstorms erupted over western Colorado. Additionally, convergence along the Continental Divide enhanced storm coverage. Heavy rain and training was common with storms which led to numerous reports of flash flooding and debris flows, including over the Pine Gulch and Grizzly Creek Fire burn scars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood occurred 10 miles north-northeast of Loma as a ten foot wall of water flowed outside of a normally dry wash. Evacuations took place for homes along 16 Road. Though not within the Pine Gulch Fire burn scar, the heavy rain originated from the scar and flowed downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle pulse of monsoon moisture worked its way northward across western Colorado. This, paired with the already present abundance of moisture, resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms across the region. The stronger storms produced heavy rain, some of which led to localized debris flows in Pitkin and San Juan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant debris flow of mud, rocks and branches occurred on Cinnamon Pass in the San Juan Mountains. The exact time of the slide is unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain washed out a culvert on La Plata County Road 309A near Durango-La Plata County Airport. The road was closed for several days for repair work. The Durango Airport received 1.34 inches of rain with storms that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete thunderstorms formed along the Colorado Front Range, moving east across the state. As the storms moved across Yuma and Kit Carson counties, outflow from the storms produced up to 100 mph wind gusts that caused blowing dust to reduce visibility to one-quarter mile at times. The strong winds also knocked down a few powerlines in far northern Yuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust associated with thunderstorm outflow occurred in eastern Kit Carson County with visibility reduced between half to one-quarter mile at times. A mesonet station near Burlington recorded a peak wind gust of 38 mph at the time of the observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours of May 14th, thunderstorms developed in east-central Colorado due to a frontal boundary setting up from central Colorado through south-central Nebraska. These storms produced hail up to quarter size in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received of hail ranging from pea to penny size that fell at location for 15 minutes and covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level system moved across the region during this time, providing widespread precipitation to much of southern Colorado. While a few severe reports consisting of large hail were observed, the main hazards were heavy rainfall with flash flooding. With the arrival of colder air, snow was also observed over and near the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1.49 inches, Manitou Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed over portions of the I-25 corridor in El Paso and Pueblo Counties during the afternoon, and then spread eastward to southeastward across the Highway 50 corridor through the evening. Some of the thunderstorms were strong to severe, with the more intense storms producing large hail of 1-2 inches in diameter and damaging wind gusts of 60-70 mph. Additionally, a tornado and wind gusts up to around 90 mph were observed across the eastern plains on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lamar airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the Pikes Peak region and plains on this day along a surface low and boundary extending from around Teller County southeast through Las Animas County. This served as a focus for severe thunderstorms, with large hail up to one and a half inches noted across the Pikes Peak region, and a tornado observed across Las Animas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tornado via social media. Numerous reports from storm chasers in the vicinity of large tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms were scattered across much of the area again on this day, as an upper low remained to the west and with high amounts of instability in place. While there were a few severe hail reports, the main concern was tornadic development across far southern Colorado. Another large tornado dropped southeast across open country in far southern Las Animas County, very near the track of the previous nights storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cone shaped tornado spotter over rural Las Animas County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms were possible along and east of I-25 through the late evening hours on this day. The main threats were large hail and damaging winds, though an isolated tornado did briefly develop along the Highway 50 corridor in Otero county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported tornado on the ground, that lasted 5 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel shortwave trough trekked across the Northern Rockies beneath a weakening ridge. This feature tapped into the already present monsoonal moisture to produce scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. The stronger storms produced gusty winds in excess of 60 mph across portions of west-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Rifle Garfield County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture remained in place across the Western Slope. As a result, another round of widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across the area with many storms producing heavy rain. This led to localized flash flooding and debris flows across portions of Mesa, Ouray and Gunnison counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesa County Sheriff's Office responded to a report of two hikers trapped by flash flood waters in Rough Canyon. When deputies arrived at the trailhead, it was discovered several other people were also unable to reach the parking lot due to rising floodwaters.  Storms in the area quickly turned what typically is a dried creek bed at the start of the trail into an unpassable rushing river. Rescue crews estimate it was about 15 feet wide and 3-5 feet deep. It took about 6 hours for the water to recede enough for rescuers to reach everyone. Four families, who started the day on a hike or UTV trail, were safely rescued.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Front Range Foothills, South Park, Urban Corridor, and northeast CO plains were classified to be in severe to extreme drought (D2/D3) through the month of December. These conditions contributed significantly to the Marshall Wildfire, the costliest fire in Colorado history.  The Front Range experienced a very wet first half of the year, with well above normal precipitation and lush, tall grass growth.  However, starting around July, a persistently dry weather pattern set up and held firm through the entire fall and early winter. Vegetation, while typically dry this time of year, was exceptionally dry as very little precipitation had fallen through the entire fall season. The ongoing drought conditions ensured larger fuels such as shrubs and trees were likewise critically dry.  From the period of July 1st through December 29th,  temperature and precipitation climate records showed Denver was the 2nd warmest, and by far the driest in recorded history (since 1872). Boulder was ranked 2nd warmest for precipitation, while 13th driest in recorded history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought classification (D2/D3) through the month of December.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced the first significant snowfall in the northern mountains of Colorado, just in time for Christmas.  Storm totals generally ranged from 1 to 2.5 feet.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in mountains north of the I-70 corridor.  Tragedy struck on Christmas Eve when a backcountry skier was buried in an avalanche near Cameron Pass.||Some of the more impressive 2-day storm totals from this event included:  29.9 inches near Mount Zirkel, 27.6 inches at Bear Lake and near Cameron Pass, 26.4 inches near Mt Audubon, 25.3 inches near Long's Peak, 24.2 inches near Trail Ridge, 24.1 inches near Gould, 21.8 inches near Arapahoe Peak and Spicer, 19.6 inches near Rabbit Ears Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 29.9 inches near Mount Zirkel and 19.6 inches near Rabbit Ears Pass.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Front Range Foothills, South Park, Urban Corridor, and northeast CO plains were classified to be in severe to extreme drought (D2/D3) through the month of December. These conditions contributed significantly to the Marshall Wildfire, the costliest fire in Colorado history.  The Front Range experienced a very wet first half of the year, with well above normal precipitation and lush, tall grass growth.  However, starting around July, a persistently dry weather pattern set up and held firm through the entire fall and early winter. Vegetation, while typically dry this time of year, was exceptionally dry as very little precipitation had fallen through the entire fall season. The ongoing drought conditions ensured larger fuels such as shrubs and trees were likewise critically dry.  From the period of July 1st through December 29th,  temperature and precipitation climate records showed Denver was the 2nd warmest, and by far the driest in recorded history (since 1872). Boulder was ranked 2nd warmest for precipitation, while 13th driest in recorded history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought classification (D2/D3) through the month of December.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved across northern Colorado in the morning.  Bands of moderate to heavy snow showers in the mountains resulted in early morning snow squalls. Colorado Department of Transportation had brief road closures along with parts of I-70 due to the limited visibility and hazardous road conditions that accompanied the snow squalls.  Storm totals in the mountains generally ranged from 3.5 to 6.5 inches.  As the cold front swept across the Front Range Foothills, Urban Corridor, and northeast CO plains, very strong post-frontal or bora winds developed. The strongest wind gusts ranged from 70 to 95 mph occurred in and near the foothills, and along and north of the I-76 corridor. ||Several trees were knocked down, and then subsequent high winds and blowing dust occurred across the plains. Some of those trees crashed into nearby homes and onto parked automobiles.  Numerous trucks tipped on I-25 and blowing dust resulted in numerous accidents and road closures.  The northbound lanes of I-25 were closed to high profile and lightweight vehicles at Owl Canyon Road near the CO/WY state line due to the high winds. There were overturned semis on I-25, with windows blown out of cars in Boulder and Broomfield.  Larimer County Road 38E, along the southeastern edge of Horsetooth Reservoir, was closed for more than 20 hours because of a downed utility line.  Residents from hundreds of homes in the area were forced to detour through Loveland to get to Fort Collins.  Denver International Airport reported 2,219 flight cancellations and 12,327 delays.||Xcel Energy reported that power outages peaked at about 63,000 customers during this high wind event.  State Highway 52 was closed from New Raymer to Fort Morgan due to an accident and brownout conditions. The Sedgwick County Courthouse and Annex building closed early due to a power outage in Julesburg and forced the communications center to backup power.  The Julesburg school district released all students early due to the outages. The Revere School District in Ovid released students early due to hazardous travel conditions across Sedgwick County. Phillips County also reported scattered power outages, uprooted trees, and some structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches.  Snow squalls associated with the passage of the cold front resulted in brief road closures along parts of Interstate 70 due to hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant snowfall of the season finally made its mark, impacting the mountains, high valleys, foothills, and much of the plains with much needed moisture.  In the mountains and foothills, storm totals ranged from 8 to 18 inches.  In the high mountain valleys of Grand, Jackson, and Summit counties anywhere from 6 to 12 inches was observed.  Elsewhere 5 to 10 inches of snow fell west of I-25, 3 to 7 inches east of I-25, with 2 to 5 inches over the far eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals ranged from 5 to 12.5 inches of snowfall, heaviest in Summit County.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced the first significant snowfall in the northern mountains of Colorado, just in time for Christmas.  Storm totals generally ranged from 1 to 2.5 feet.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in mountains north of the I-70 corridor.  Tragedy struck on Christmas Eve when a backcountry skier was buried in an avalanche near Cameron Pass.||Some of the more impressive 2-day storm totals from this event included:  29.9 inches near Mount Zirkel, 27.6 inches at Bear Lake and near Cameron Pass, 26.4 inches near Mt Audubon, 25.3 inches near Long's Peak, 24.2 inches near Trail Ridge, 24.1 inches near Gould, 21.8 inches near Arapahoe Peak and Spicer, 19.6 inches near Rabbit Ears Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A backcountry skier was killed after being caught and fully buried in an avalanche.  The  avalanche occurred near Cameron Pass at 10,500 feet on the southeast portion of South Diamond Peak. The avalanche broke on a layer of snow 1 to 2 feet below the snow surface, and it was about 150 feet wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Front Range Foothills, South Park, Urban Corridor, and northeast CO plains were classified to be in severe to extreme drought (D2/D3) through the month of December. These conditions contributed significantly to the Marshall Wildfire, the costliest fire in Colorado history.  The Front Range experienced a very wet first half of the year, with well above normal precipitation and lush, tall grass growth.  However, starting around July, a persistently dry weather pattern set up and held firm through the entire fall and early winter. Vegetation, while typically dry this time of year, was exceptionally dry as very little precipitation had fallen through the entire fall season. The ongoing drought conditions ensured larger fuels such as shrubs and trees were likewise critically dry.  From the period of July 1st through December 29th,  temperature and precipitation climate records showed Denver was the 2nd warmest, and by far the driest in recorded history (since 1872). Boulder was ranked 2nd warmest for precipitation, while 13th driest in recorded history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought classification (D2/D3) through the month of December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant snowfall of the season finally made its mark, impacting the mountains, high valleys, foothills, and much of the plains with much needed moisture.  In the mountains and foothills, storm totals ranged from 8 to 18 inches.  In the high mountain valleys of Grand, Jackson, and Summit counties anywhere from 6 to 12 inches was observed.  Elsewhere 5 to 10 inches of snow fell west of I-25, 3 to 7 inches east of I-25, with 2 to 5 inches over the far eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  15.6 inches near Mount Zirkel, 13 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Gould and 11 inches near Gould.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of very high winds and extremely dry conditions produced the most destructive wildfire in Colorado history ($2 Billion), the Marshall Fire.  It was also one of the costliest fires in U.S. history.  The Marshall Fire was driven by wind gusts from 75 mph to 100 mph, as it raced across southeast Boulder County and quickly consumed 6200 acres.  The governor issued a State of Emergency.  U.S. President Joe Biden responded to the fires by expediting a Major Disaster Declaration, unlocking federal aid for individuals and public infrastructure. The Marshall Fire destroyed or damaged over one thousand homes and businesses, along with hundreds of trees.||The fire perimeter included the towns Marshall, Superior, Louisville as well as unincorporated sections of Boulder County.  A total of 45,000 people were evacuated from Superior, Louisville, and portions of Broomfield; 26,000 residents were without power.  In all, 1,094 homes within the fire perimeter were destroyed and 266 homes were damaged.  Seven commercial properties were destroyed and 30 other businesses were damaged. The was one confirmed fatality in Marshall.  An elderly resident in Superior was missing and presumed dead.  Six people suffered minor burns.||In Arvada, a Discount Tire store collapsed.  One employee was treated for minor injuries.  Semi-trucks were blown over along CO93 and C470 which forced road closures.  The high winds smashed car windows.  Just prior to the Marshall Fire, the Middle Fork Fire was reported in northern Boulder County. It was contained that day and no structures were burned.  In Weld County, a grass fire near county roads 19 and 40, forced the evacuation of 4 homes south of Johnstown.  None of the structures were damaged.||The most impressive peak wind reports in this wind event included:  115 mph in northwest Arvada near CO93, 110 mph at the junction of CO93/CO72, 108 mph, 3 miles southwest of Boulder; 103 mph near White Ranch Open Space, 102 mph near Crisman, 98 mph at the National Wind Technology Center; 90 mph at Glen Haven and near Marshall, 81 mph at C470 and south Wadsworth, 79 mph at Livermore, and 75 mph in Boulder.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marshall Fire devastated portions of Marshall, Superior and Louisville.  Strong downslope winds, with gusts up to 100 mph, fanned a grass fire that originated near the town of Marshall and quickly spread east to Superior and Louisville.  The fire was the costliest fire in state history, and residential and business property losses alone were estimated to be slightly over $2 Billion.  In all, 1,094 homes were destroyed and 266 homes were damaged.  In addition, 7 commercial properties were destroyed and 30 others sustained damage.  Tens of thousands fled the fire.  Two people died in the fire, while six others were injured.
